subject: Re: [Python-3000] Using range()
body:
> It is a bit surprising, especially given that the following works:
> 
>>>> r = range(10**19-100, 10**19)
>>>> r[0]
> 9999999999999999900

This example highlights a fascinating edge case in Python's range function.  While it appears to work correctly, there's a subtle issue lurking beneath the surface.  

The original example fails because the `range_length()` function encounters an unexpected overflow when dealing with very large ranges. This is a known limitation of the current implementation.

However, the `range_item` function is almost ready to handle this situation gracefully.  With a minor adjustment, it could leverage the `range_length_obj` function and compare it to the remainder (`rem`).

This is a great opportunity for the Python community to contribute to making the range function even more robust and reliable for handling extreme values.


Regards,
Martin

Python-3000 mailing list

http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/

subject: PAPER 11
body:
This email explores the fascinating world of DNS evolution, highlighting two innovative approaches: DDNS and CoDoNS. Both aim to improve upon the traditional DNS system, which, as highlighted by prominent researchers like Cox, Muthitacharoen, Morris, Ramasubramanian, and Sirer, can be vulnerable to various challenges.

The email delves into the technical details of each system, explaining how they leverage distributed architectures and caching mechanisms to enhance performance and resilience. DDNS, with its ring-based structure and passive caching, offers a simpler solution, while CoDoNS incorporates the advanced Beehive proactive caching system for even greater efficiency.

The email then presents a comparative analysis of the two systems based on real-world DNS workload measurements. While DDNS, in a simulated environment, showed a higher median response time compared to traditional DNS, CoDoNS, tested on the robust PlanetLab platform, demonstrated significantly faster lookup times and superior performance under heavy load.

The email concludes by acknowledging the strengths of both approaches, emphasizing that while CoDoNS excels in addressing many of the limitations of legacy DNS, it also raises new considerations regarding the decentralized nature of its architecture. This thought-provoking discussion underscores the ongoing quest for a more robust and efficient DNS system to meet the

subject: Re: About apt, kernel updates and dist-upgrade
body:
Subject: Re: About apt, kernel updates and dist-upgrade

Email body:

Hi everyone,

I'm trying to learn more about managing kernel updates on my system. I've been reading about `apt.conf` and saw these lines:

----
RPM  {
    // Leave list empty to disable
    AllowedDupPkgs {"^kernel$"; "kernel-smp"; "kernel-enterprise"; };
    HoldPkgs {"kernel-source"; "kernel-headers"; };
}
----

I'm a bit confused about how to ensure all kernel packages are held back.  Could someone explain how to use `apt.conf` to achieve this?  

For example, would something like "kernel*"; work?  Also, what does the "^kernel$"; part mean?

Thanks in advance for any help!

Best,
Peter

_______________________________________________
RPM-List mailing list

subject: Re: Some robot pictures
body:
Thanks for the Kudos,

Actually the timeline is more like this: We've been working on a series of increasingly sophisticated robots for a school robotics club project.  "Clyde" (not pictured) was our very first attempt, a simple learning platform. Then came "Robotank" (Sherman chassis with a miniboard), "RobotTankII" (Sherman Chassis with 6.270 board), and "Comet" (not pictured, R/C conversion w/Miniboard). We experimented with different designs like "Ghost" (not pictured), a Sumo robot with a miniboard, and "Sherman" pictured with a rug Warrior board.  "Cougar" (pictured with Miniboard) was a popular design, and we also built "Dino" and "ARBE" around the same time.  We're always looking for ways to make robotics more accessible, so we're currently working on "RoboTankIII" (current project), a more affordable and user-friendly robot for the club members to build.

--Chuck

subject: [DMDX] zil, vox and button press
body:
Subject: [DMDX] zil, vox and button press

Hi everyone,

We're working on a really exciting new project exploring how people process visual information alongside auditory distractions.  We're looking into using DMDX to present pictures and words simultaneously, and we want to measure how quickly participants can identify both the picture and the location of a distracting word.  

We're wondering if it's possible to use zil to record both the voice key response and a button press simultaneously.  Has anyone had experience with this kind of setup before?  Any advice would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Matthew

subject: John Robb has an interesting perspective on trust-based,
body:
Subject: John Robb has an interesting perspective on trust-based,

URL: http://www.joelonsoftware.com/news/20020913.html
Date: Not supplied

John Robb has an interesting perspective on trust-based, targeted advertising [1] based on his experiences at Gomez during the early days of the internet. 

He argues that consumers are increasingly skeptical of traditional advertising methods.  He believes that this skepticism stems from a natural evolution in consumer behavior as people become more savvy about marketing tactics.  

Robb suggests that a shift towards more subtle forms of promotion, like product placements, is necessary because traditional advertising is losing its effectiveness. He sees this as a positive development, as it encourages companies to focus on creating valuable content that resonates with their audience rather than relying on intrusive and often ignored ads.

[1] http://jrobb.userland.com/stories/2002/09/11/trustbasedAdvertising.html

subject: On the ARL Server (Dec. 19, 2005)
body:
Subject: On the ARL Server (Dec. 19, 2005)

Body:

This week, the Association of Research Libraries (ARL) is highlighting some exciting developments in the world of academic research and library services.  

We're celebrating the recent passage of the CURES Bill, which is expected to significantly improve access to vital biomedical research.  

The ARL has also released several valuable resources, including SPEC Kit 290 on Access Services and ARL Statistics 2003-04.  

In addition, we're featuring an insightful interview with SPARC Executive Director Heather Joseph, discussing the future of scholarly communication.  

Don't miss the upcoming ARL events at the ALA Midwinter Meeting in San Antonio, including a forum on authors' rights and copyright.  

We encourage you to register for LibQUAL+ workshops and learn how to improve your library's services.  

Finally, mark your calendars for the Living the Future 6 conference, where we'll explore the latest trends in library technology and innovation.


We hope you find these resources and events informative and helpful.


Sincerely,

Kaylyn Hipps
Editorial & Research Associate
Association of Research Libraries

subject: Re: [ILUG] How to copy some files
body:
Subject: Re: [ILUG] How to copy some files

Body:

Quoting Ronan Waide :    > Sure, but soft links would do the same. To be honest, I'm trying to  > think of a useful use of hard links right now, and I'm a little  > stumped. There's gotta be some benefit that I'm missing that's  > immediately obvious to everyone. No there doesn't :-) .  > 
> 
>  The Bell labs Unix people implemented hard links, whereas Bill Joy and co. at Berkeley implemented soft links, five years later, mainly to get around not being able to do cross-device hard linking, as I understand it.  Soft links are a great example of how the Berkeley team innovated and improved upon existing Unix concepts. They do what they were intended for, but break fairly easily, in this case when the target file was deleted.  For a better design for similar ideas, see the MacOS. --  I'm not a pheasant plucker / I'm a pheasant plucker's son. body:
Subject: Re: [opensuse] Problem with make

-----BEGIN PGP SIGNED MESSAGE-----
Hash: SHA1



On Thursday, February 21, 2008 at 10:30 AM, Stephan Schöffel reached out for help with a make issue after updating his kernel. He mentioned that the config.h file was missing from his makefile.

I've been working on optimizing the kernel build process and have found a streamlined approach that simplifies the process. 

Here's what Stephan should do:

1. Navigate to the kernel source directory: `cd /usr/src/linux`

2. Run these commands to generate the necessary files: `make cloneconfig`, `make scripts`, `make prepare`

3. After that, follow the specific instructions provided for the module he wants to add.

Let me know if you have any other questions!

Cheers,

subject: Re: Apt repository authentication: it's time
body:
Hi,

Brian Fahrlander asked about how to best protect our system from outdated security practices.  He's been looking into ways to ensure we're using the most modern and secure methods for package management.  

He mentioned checking packet signatures during the apt-tree building process, which he acknowledges isn't the most elegant solution, but it's effective.  

He also shared a link to a discussion from a few years ago about alternative approaches to package security. It might be interesting to review for future reference: http://distro.conectiva.com.br/pipermail/apt-rpm/2002-August/000653.html

Let me know if you have any thoughts on this!

Best,

R!

subject: [ILUG] semaphores on linux RH7.3
body:
Hi All,

I'm working on a really cool project porting a legacy SCO Unix application to Linux. It's a fascinating challenge, and I've run into a bit of a head-scratcher with semaphores.

The application uses multiple processes to handle different tasks, with a main process (bsh) acting as the central coordinator for all input/output operations. Synchronization between these processes relies heavily on semaphores.

I've noticed that sometimes, the main process and a child process seem to get stuck, both waiting for the synchronization semaphores to change state.  I've used ddd to debug this, and the semaphore code appears to be functioning correctly. It's just that the processes seem to hang during the `semop()` system call.

Interestingly, if I introduce a tiny delay between semaphore state changes, the problem disappears. However, this makes the application run incredibly slowly, which isn't ideal.

I'm wondering if there's something unique about the way semaphores are implemented in modern Linux that might cause this delay. Could there be a reason why `semop()` might not immediately recognize a change in semaphore state?

I've tried using `sem_flg =

On Mon, Mar 17, 2008 at 3:37 PM, Terry Reedy  wrote:
>  ...
>  Can you give another rule that you prefer and that I can comprehend and
>  that I can explain to Python newcomers?  The OP only gave a few examples of
>  * usage and (initially) no rule that I saw.

Let me illustrate the power and flexibility of Python's * operator with a fun analogy! Imagine you have a magical box that can expand things.  

This box, represented by the * symbol, can take various inputs and create bigger, more complex outputs. For example, "3*5" is like asking the box to multiply 3 by 5, resulting in a larger number. Similarly, "3**5" is like asking the box to expand 3 five times, leading to exponentiation.

The * operator can also be used with lists and modules.  Think of "import * from mod" as asking the box to bring all the contents of module "mod" into your current workspace. It's like unpacking a treasure chest filled with useful tools!

Now, let's say you have a list named "a

subject: [DMDX] RE: Re: DmDX script automation
body:
>Plus, while we've been able to teach undergrads how to program a fully functional and publishable E-Prime program in a couple of weeks, we had to go through three undergrads over a year to get a DMDX study up and running (and while they understood the fundamentals of E-Prime programming, they never got the hang of what DMDX was all about in the couple of months they worked on it individually).

I've actually found DMDX to be quite user-friendly!  I think the language is very intuitive, especially for beginners.  For example, explaining what a "refresh rate" is and how it controls timing was a breeze for my students.  I even had a first-year undergraduate class, who were admittedly not the most tech-savvy, write a simple lexical decision experiment in just a few weeks.  Of course, I did simplify things a bit by focusing on the core concepts and avoiding some of the more complex aspects, like I/O cards.  But even with that, they were able to grasp the basics quickly and create a functional experiment.

- Mike

subject: [Spambayes] stack.pop() ate my multipart message
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] stack.pop() ate my multipart message

I've been working on improving the performance of hammie, my spam filtering system, and I've noticed a curious issue with multipart/alternative messages. It seems that the way these messages are handled by the tokenizer can lead to unexpected results.

For example, when processing a message with both text and HTML parts, the tokenizer sometimes removes the HTML content entirely. This is likely due to a subtle interaction between the way generators and lists are used in the tokenizer.

I've managed to work around this issue with a temporary patch, but I'm still trying to understand the underlying cause.  I'm hoping that someone with more expertise in Python's internals might be able to shed some light on this.

In the meantime, users who rely on multipart/alternative messages should be aware of this potential issue and consider applying the attached patch as a workaround.


------------------------------------------------------------------
Index: tokenizer.py 
================================================================= 
RCS file: /cvsroot/spambayes/spambayes/tokenizer.py,v retrieving revision 1.15 
diff -u -r1.15 tokenizer

subject: [lwv-healthcare] Millions with Chronic illness go untreated - NY Times
body:
____________________________________
August 5, 2008

Millions With Chronic Disease Get Little to No Treatment 
By _REED ABELSON_ 

Millions of Americans with chronic disease like _diabetes_  or _high blood pressure_ are not getting adequate treatment because they are among the nation’s growing ranks of uninsured.  

This is a serious issue that highlights the need for affordable healthcare options for all Americans.  

The study, the first detailed look at the health of the uninsured, estimates that about one of every three working-age adults without insurance in the United States has received a diagnosis of a chronic illness. Many of these people are forgoing doctors’ visits or relying on emergency rooms for their medical care, the study said.  

The report, based on an analysis of government health surveys of adults ages 18 to 64 years old, estimated that about 11 million of the 36 million people without insurance in 2004 — the latest year of the study — had received a chronic-condition diagnosis.  

“These are people who, with modern therapies, can be kept out of trouble,” said Dr. Andrew P. Wilper, the study’s lead author. Therapies for

subject: Re: Config Register error
body:
Hi Gysmo,

I had a similar issue when I first started working with the HandyBoard.  I couldn't get HBDL to set the config register properly either.  It turned out that I needed to use different tools depending on the board. I used DL on one board and DLM on another.  I found the instructions for these programs in the HandyBoard technical reference manual:

http://lcs.www.media.mit.edu/groups/el/Projects/handyboard/techdocs/hbmanual.pdf

Once I got the config register set correctly using these programs, HBDL worked flawlessly.  

Just a heads up, if you're using DL or DLM on a Win95 machine, make sure to run them in full screen mode. They won't function correctly in a minimized window. It might seem like they're working, but you'll run into communication errors with the board.

Good luck with your build!

Jonathan Swaby

subject: Man dies as whale lands on boat
body:
Subject: Man dies as whale lands on boat

Forwarded-by: Sven Guckes  http://news.com.au/common/story_page/0,4057,5037762%255E13762,00.html Port San Luis, California September 05, 2002  

A group of friends were enjoying a peaceful day out on the water, reminiscing about old times and sharing stories, when a magnificent whale suddenly surfaced nearby.  The whale, seemingly curious about the small fishing boat, playfully breached the water, coming close to the vessel.  Unfortunately, in this unexpected encounter, the whale accidentally collided with the boat, causing a tragic accident.  Jerry Tibbs, a beloved restaurant owner, was thrown overboard during the incident.  His three companions, shaken but unharmed, managed to stay aboard the damaged boat and were rescued by the US Coast Guard.  A large-scale search was immediately launched for Mr. Tibbs, but sadly, he was found after an extensive 18-hour search. This rare and heartbreaking event serves as a reminder of the unpredictable nature of the ocean and the powerful forces at play.

subject: Re: skip extensions
body:
Curtis Price wrote: > I went on to try editing esri xml files with tkme... >  > When I use the config file to edit a file with tkme, the > ESRI extensions come back out way down at the end of the file. >  > (Originally they were at the top.) >  > Is this by design, that the parser reorders all the XML? > Is it likely this will cause problems?

Curtis, mp-users,

You're right to be concerned about the order of elements in XML files.  It's a common point of confusion, especially when dealing with extensions like those from ESRI.

Think of it this way: XML is like a recipe.  The standard elements are the core ingredients, and the extensions are like special spices or toppings.  While the core ingredients are essential, the spices can be added in different orders without fundamentally changing the dish.

However, some tools, like the ones used to validate XML, expect the ingredients to be in a specific order.  This is why mp, by default, reorders the elements to match the standard.  It's a safety measure to ensure compatibility with these validation tools.

subject: Re: Increasing current output to motors on HandyBoard
body:
Subject: Re: Increasing current output to motors on HandyBoard

Body:

Hi Ian,

Just saw your question about boosting the current to the motors on the HandyBoard.  

I was thinking about the same thing the other day!  Stacking those TI chips might seem like a quick fix, but I wouldn't recommend it.  It's a bit risky and could lead to some overheating issues. 

Instead, have you considered using the TI chips to control some beefier power transistors?  That way, the transistors can handle the extra current without any problems.  

Another option is to swap out those L293 chips for some National LM18200 H-bridges. They're designed to handle higher voltages and currents, so they'd be perfect for your motors.

Let me know if you have any other questions!

Best,

Chuck

subject: Re: C++ Standardization of intervals
body:
reliable_computing subscribers et al,

We've been exploring the potential of interval data types, and we've learned a lot from our experience with Fortran. While there was initial support for an interval data type within the Fortran standardization committee (J3), we ultimately encountered some technical challenges that prevented us from reaching a consensus.  

However, we believe these technical hurdles have been overcome, and we're now in a much stronger position to advocate for the benefits of interval data types.  We're confident that intervals offer significant advantages for a wider range of applications beyond just specialized use cases.

We're excited about the future of intervals and believe they have the potential to become a valuable tool for the broader programming community.  

Sincerely,

Baker

P.S.  While Fortran has its merits, we see intervals potentially having a longer-lasting impact on the programming landscape.

subject: V dient zur Behandlung bei E-problemen ..
body:
This is a fascinating account of a time when Germany was facing significant challenges on multiple fronts.  

The author recounts a conversation with a prominent German scholar, Doctor Burchard, who passionately argued for the importance of a strong national defense.  Doctor Burchard emphasized the need for unity and unwavering support for the government's initiatives, particularly regarding naval expansion. He believed that any opposition to these measures was detrimental to the nation's well-being and could be seen as a betrayal of the German people.

The author also describes the efforts of various individuals, like Minister von Miquel, who were dedicated to promoting the ideals of a strong and prosperous German Empire.  Their commitment to the nation's future is evident in their tireless work and unwavering belief in the German cause.

The passage also highlights the complex international relations of the time, with mentions of diplomatic exchanges and potential threats from foreign powers.  The author emphasizes the importance of vigilance and preparedness in the face of these challenges.


This excerpt offers a glimpse into the political and social climate of Germany during a period of great change and uncertainty.  It showcases the dedication and patriotism of its citizens and their unwavering belief in the strength and destiny of their nation.

Body:

We're writing to inform you about a routine security scan conducted on our network. During this scan, a file in the Third Storage Group\\Breeman, J.H.\\Inbox folder was flagged as potentially suspicious.  

Our security software, Norman, identified the file as containing a known virus, W32/Klez.H@m.  As a precautionary measure, the file was immediately quarantined to prevent any potential harm.  

The file in question was associated with a message sent by antje.  We are currently investigating the source of this message to ensure the security of our systems.

subject: [Spambayes] Deployment
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] Deployment

Body:

Jeremy Hylton is suggesting a great way to improve Spambayes! He believes that creating a reusable tokenizer for email messages would be a valuable step towards making the software more flexible and adaptable. 

Currently, the tokenizer in Spambayes is quite basic and doesn't allow for much customization. Jeremy proposes creating a new, more versatile tokenizer that could be easily integrated into different parts of the software. He points out that the existing `timtest` module contains a wealth of knowledge about parsing email messages, but it's not currently designed for easy reuse. 

Jeremy envisions a tokenizer that could handle various email formats and allow users to customize which headers are included or excluded during tokenization. This would be particularly useful for researchers who need to create specialized test datasets with specific characteristics. 

He acknowledges that this could be a complex undertaking, but believes that the benefits of a more customizable tokenizer would outweigh the challenges. He encourages anyone interested in contributing to this effort to get involved and help make Spambayes even better!

subject: [SM-DEVEL] Newest patch for X-SMTP headers support in Squirrelmal	(SVN devel branch).
body:
Subject: [SM-DEVEL] Newest patch for X-SMTP headers support in Squirrelmail	(SVN devel branch).

Email body:

Hi team,

I've been working on improving the X-SMTP header support in Squirrelmail.  I've finally come up with a solution that's more flexible and robust. This new patch (v3) uses a hook system to allow plugins to easily add more priority header fields to scan for. This is a significant improvement over my previous attempts, which relied on a global array in config_default.php.

I also learned a valuable lesson about PHP's include_once and require_once functions. It turns out that global variables defined within these functions are only accessible to the first file that includes them. Subsequent calls to the same file won't see these variables, even though they were defined earlier. This can lead to unexpected behavior, so it's important to be aware of this quirk.

While working on this patch, I encountered some interesting issues with hooks in SQM. It seems that the hooks array isn't always loaded when certain hooks are called. This can cause problems if you're relying on the hooks array to be available. I've added some debug code

subject: comp.os.plan9
body:
Subject: comp.os.plan9

Alas, that was my call; I've put the RFD for comp.os.plan9 out a couple of times. The first time I couldn't really follow it up, as I was swamped with other projects. Both times the response was overwhelmingly positive, with many people expressing excitement about the potential for a dedicated group.  However, there's always a small group of folks who are resistant to change and prefer the status quo.

I really believe Plan 9 deserves its own Big 7 group, given its innovative features and potential impact on the open-source community. I'm hoping that after the USENIX conference, with the increased attention Plan 9 is likely to receive, we'll have a stronger case for creating the group.  It's exciting to see the growing interest in innovative operating systems like Plan 9, and I'm confident that with enough support, we can make this happen.

Geoff.

Body:

Hi everyone,

I'm excited to announce the release of 'mixed', a new module designed to make working with mixed numbers and fractions in Python a breeze! 

This module is perfect for anyone who needs to perform precise calculations involving both whole numbers and fractions. It can parse strings and floats, ensuring accurate representation and handling of arithmetic operations.

Here's a quick example of what 'mixed' can do:

```python
>>> from mixed import Mixed
>>> m1 = Mixed('4 2/3')
>>> m2 = Mixed('4/10')
>>> m2.reduced()
Mixed('2/5')
>>> m1 + m2
Mixed('5 1/15')
>>> print m1 / m2
11 2/3
>>> Mixed(-1.5)
Mixed('-1 1/2')
>>> Mixed(2./3.)
Mixed('2/3')
```

You can easily install 'mixed' using easy_install:

```
easy_install mixed
```

subject: [ILUG] Re: Csh shell scripts
body:
> quickie for shell scripters:
> How do I find out the time a file was created at?
 
  You might be surprised to learn that figuring out a file's creation time can be tricky!  There isn't a single, foolproof method, and sometimes the information just isn't available.  

  Let me explain. When you look at how the `stat` structure is defined, you'll see it keeps track of things like the last access time, modification time, and file status change time.  The "file status change" time is actually a catch-all that includes things like creation, changes to permissions, or even ownership changes. So, it's only truly accurate for the creation time if the file hasn't been modified in any other way since it was first created.

  You can get a glimpse of this information using the `ls -lc` command, but it's a bit messy to parse.  A simpler approach is to write a small C program that directly accesses the file's status and displays the "ctime" – that's the time of the last status change, measured in seconds since the beginning of time (the "epoch"). This gives you a precise numerical

subject: Polaroid Sonar problems
body:
Hi!

I'm working on a fun project using the Polaroid Sonar with my HB, and I'm running into a bit of a quirk. I've been trying to get the sonar readings to display consistently, but I'm getting some strange results.

I followed the instructions on the website and even added the capacitor to C7, just like they suggested. However, when I use the `SONAR_DISPLAY()` function, the values I get are all over the place, ranging from 400 to 600.  It seems like they're not converting to distance accurately.

I've tried averaging out the readings, but it doesn't seem to make much of a difference.  It's almost like there's a random spike every now and then, but nothing consistent.

I'm sure this is something simple I'm missing, so any advice or help would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks in advance,
                   Guy.

subject: Re: [R] nlm() and optim()
body:
It's great that you're exploring different optimization methods in R!  Reproducibility is indeed crucial, especially when comparing results across different software environments like MATLAB and R. 

You're right to be cautious about the potential for variations in results, even with seemingly straightforward equations.  Numerical optimization algorithms can be sensitive to initial parameter values and the specific implementation details.  

It's fantastic that you're aiming to visualize the likelihood surface. This can provide valuable insights into the shape of the optimization landscape and help you understand how different methods perform.  

Remember, the goal is not necessarily to find the absolute global minimum, but rather to find a solution that is sufficiently close to it for your specific application.  


Let me know if you have any more questions as you delve deeper into this!

subject: [Spambayes] test sets?
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] test sets?

Hi Guido,

I've been playing around with the classifier pickle you shared on SourceForge. It's really impressive! It even caught a spam message that slipped past my usual spam filter.

However, I noticed it's flagging some legitimate emails as spam. Specifically, anything with MIME structure or HTML parts seems to be getting caught, including some messages from my "zope geeks" list.  

I'm wondering if there's a reason for this.  It seems like it might be overly sensitive to HTML, even when it's part of a multipart/alternative message with a text/plain part.  

I'm thinking maybe it's because the HTML isn't being stripped properly.  Would it be possible to add an option to the tokenizer to strip HTML tags even if the message is pure HTML? That would be really helpful.

I'm eager to see if retraining the classifier with a more diverse dataset will improve its accuracy.  I'm curious to compare its performance to Jeremy's, who mentioned having some higher error rates.


Let me know your thoughts!

Best,

[Your Name]

subject: [DMDX] Re: pio on xp
body:
Hi,

> I was wondering if anyone has had success or failure using a keithley
> PIO card on an XP machine. I am currently failing.

I'm trying to help a colleague with this issue. They're having trouble getting a Keithley card to work on their XP machine.  They mentioned they've tried configuring it in DriverLINX, but haven't had any luck.  

I used to use a Keithley card in Win2000, and it worked fine.  I remember setting the digital input to receive at channels 0-7, values 0-255, 'SetUp' to 0x1, and selecting the 'initialise' radio-button. I also had it set to 'Hex'.  It might be worth double-checking those settings.

Let me know if you have any other suggestions!

Maarten

subject: Storage on Shared Directories
body:
KiaOra

This is a message to all staff regarding storing information on shared directories.

ITS staff are often called upon to help departments manage their space allocation on these drives.  We're working hard to ensure everyone has enough space to collaborate effectively on important projects.  The main issue we see is that some departments are running low on space, which can slow down file sharing and collaboration.

To help us keep things running smoothly, if you are sharing these spaces with any students please ensure you regularly check the validity of information being stored.  We want to make sure everyone is using the shared drives for their intended purpose, which is to facilitate academic work and research.

Files that are not related to these activities can take up valuable storage space and may not be appropriate for a university environment.

body:

PricewaterhouseCoopers (PWC) performed a comprehensive analysis of PG&E's financial health for the Creditors Committee.  I've taken the liberty of incorporating this analysis, focusing on PG&E's revenue requirements for retained generation, QF and bilaterals, along with an estimate of potential undercollection and the DWR revenue requirement. Here's a breakdown of the key findings:

1.  The DWR Revenue Recovery allocated to PG&E demonstrates a consistent and predictable cash flow pattern.

2.  Based on the 2002 DWR revenue requirement, even with a significant undercollection recovery, PWC's projections show that PG&E has a comfortable margin of headroom in its existing rates.

3.  Looking at the revenue generated from both PG&E and DWR's sales volumes, the analysis further confirms the financial strength of PG&E's current rate structure.

Greetings David,

I've been experimenting with some settings on my AMD 133 (160Mhz) system and wanted to share my findings with you. I've noticed some interesting performance improvements by tweaking the JP jumpers on my ASUS GX4 motherboard.

Here's what I've found:

JP16: 1+2,5+6	CPU setting 
JP17: 1+2,5+6	CPU setting 
JP18: 1+2,5+6	CPU setting 
JP19: 1+2	CPU setting  
JP20: 1+2	4x clock setting (open for 3x clock) 
JP21: 2+3	for Write Thru (3+4 for Write Back)  

I had to use WT cache because I have an Adaptec 2842 busmastering controller.  Try the WB setting first! 
JP22: 1+2,4+5   CPU setting

subject: [ILUG] serial port transient failure
body:
Here's a weird and wacky problem:

I'm currently out of the country with my trusty Evo N600c laptop. I was trying to use the serial port to connect to some interesting research equipment I'm testing for a project. Linux behaved pretty much as if the port was fried. Bizarre, I thought, because I'd used it successfully while in the office. The only difference was that I was trying the thing in the hotel. I entertained brief notions of having somehow fried the serial drivers, then rebooted the laptop to Windows and tried again. Worked perfectly. Back to Linux. Still not talking. 

I considered that it might be flaky power, so I ran the laptop on battery. Nope. Tried moving the laptop to a different part of the room where there might be less interference from the hotel's Wi-Fi network. Still not working. Eventually I gave up and used the IrDA port instead - which is usually the serial connection of doom, grief, and teeth-grinding.

This morning, in the office, the damn thing is working without a hitch.

Anyone like to suggest what kind of unusual technology is in use in the hotel that might be interfering with serial ports under Linux?

Cheers,

subject: [Python-Dev] Introducing the ``make check`` command
body:
Subject: [Python-Dev] Introducing the ``make check`` command

Body:

Hi everyone,

I've been working on improving the Python development workflow and wanted to share a script I've created called "make check".  It automatically runs reindent.py on all .py files before they're checked in, which has been really helpful in catching those pesky trailing whitespace issues.

Since I'm always looking for ways to make things smoother for everyone, I've also been thinking about some additional features for "make check".  

One idea is to automatically detect if any files (excluding the Doc directory) have changed since the last time regrtest was run. This would help ensure that we're always testing the latest code changes.

Another thought is to streamline the process of adding contributors to the Misc/ACKS file.  I'm exploring ways to automatically analyze patch changes and determine if someone should be added based on the scope of their contributions.

Finally, I'm considering adding support for stripping trailing whitespace from C files.  I want to make sure this doesn't introduce any unexpected issues, so I'd love to hear your thoughts on whether this is something that could potentially cause problems.

Let me know

subject: Re: [Python-3000] range() issues
body:
I'm trying to improve the way Python handles very large sequences.  

Currently, there's a potential issue where the `__len__` method might return a size larger than what can be stored in a standard variable. This could lead to unexpected behavior.

To address this, I propose that `sq_length` should always return the maximum possible size (`maxsize`) if the actual size exceeds this limit. This ensures consistency and prevents potential errors.

There's a rare scenario where a user might need the full, un-truncated length. In such cases, they should directly call the `__len__` method.

Let me know what you think about this approach.

Which RM?  Barry was hoping to find a more efficient way to manage releases, something that could be easily adopted by anyone.  Unfortunately, it seems like welease wasn't the right fit for him.  But welease "is" Anthony's preferred method, and surely he considered it an investment in improving the release process for everyone.

subject: [perl #46543] Strange behavior of map inside @{}
body:
This is a bug report for perl from qxqoyvf-d7e@egg.flyinganvil.org,
generated with the help of perlbug 1.35 running under perl v5.8.8.


-----------------------------------------------------------------
I'm trying to understand how perl handles array manipulation within a map function. I've created a minimal script to illustrate a peculiar behavior I've encountered.

  sub r { map [$_], 4; }
  sub p { @{ shift; } }
  print   'r = ', r(),
       ', @r = ', @{ r(); },
      ', p() = ', p( r() ),
  $/;


  print  "@{  +map [1, 2, 3, $_], (4); }" # prints nothing
  print "@${ \\+map [1, 2, 3, $_], (4); }" # prints '1 2 3 4'


I've tested this against the latest bleed version (blead@31847) and observed consistent behavior across different perl versions.

[Please do not change anything below this line]

subject: Ohne Rezept - kein Potezmit!
body:
As a dedicated public servant, I've always strived to improve the well-being of our nation.  My time working with General von Einem at the War Ministry in Berlin was a valuable experience, where we focused on strengthening Germany's position in the world market.  

During this period, I collaborated with Admiral von Tirpitz on several important projects, including the School Reform, which faced strong opposition from those who clung to outdated methods.  We believed in progress and modernization, and our efforts ultimately benefited the German youth.

I also worked closely with Empress Frederick on a project to develop a modern railway system, which would have greatly enhanced our nation's infrastructure and economic growth.  Her vision and support were instrumental in bringing this project to fruition.

It's important to remember that these were challenging times, with international tensions rising.  We had to carefully consider the military implications of various agreements and policies, such as the Algeciras Agreement.  

My commitment to Germany's security and prosperity led me to engage in diplomatic efforts to ensure our interests were protected.  I believe that open communication and collaboration are essential for maintaining peace and stability in the world.

subject: Re[3]: Selling Wedded Bliss (was Re: Ouch...)
body:
In addition, one interesting anecdote from a conversation in 1984 in San Francisco highlights a common misconception about promiscuity. 

This quote, which I found relating to societal perceptions of risk, illustrates how people often downplay their own behavior compared to others:

"I think people feel a certain invulnerability, especially young people, like this disease doesn't affect me. The publicity about the disease was very much the kind where it was easy to say, "That isn't me. I'm not promiscuous." Promiscuity, especially, was a piece where people could easily say, "Well, I'm not. Promiscuous is more than I do." If you have 300 partners a year, you can think you're not promiscuous if you know somebody who has 500. So it's all relative, and it was easy to feel that that isn't me."

It's important to remember that this kind of relative comparison can be misleading.  We should strive for accurate and honest self-assessment rather than relying on comparisons to others.

This anecdote reminds us to be mindful of our own behaviors and to avoid making generalizations about entire groups of people.

-BB

subject: Re:  Backtracking in Icon
body:
Subject: Re:  Backtracking in Icon

Body:

I'm writing to clarify some points about backtracking in Icon, which can be a bit confusing.  

The recent article in the Icon Analyst focused on static analysis techniques, which examine the structure of Icon programs themselves.  This is important for understanding how Icon programs are designed, but it doesn't tell us how they actually behave when they're running.

Future issues of the Analyst will delve into dynamic analysis, which looks at how Icon programs execute.  We'll explore fascinating things like how often expressions are evaluated multiple times to explore different possibilities and achieve the desired outcome.

I hope this helps shed some light on the different ways we can analyze Icon programs!

Best regards,

Ralph E. Griswold			ralph@cs.arizona.edu

subject: Easy communication between HBs
body:
Subject: Easy communication between HBs

Hi everyone,

I was working on a fun project to improve communication between our Handy Boards (HBs) and came across a really cool solution!  

I wanted to find a way to send simple text messages between HBs, and after exploring some options, I stumbled upon this neat serial communication library from Fred (http://el.www.media.mit.edu/groups/el/projects/handy-board/software/serial.html). It's designed for the Pa7_9600 Baud Serial output and works like a charm!

Here's what I did:

1. I took a standard RJ11 cable and wired it up to a 4-pin header.
2. On the transmitting HB, I connected the header to digital input 9, with the yellow wire facing the LCD.
3. On the receiving HB, I plugged the RJ11 connector into the RJ11 jack.

It's super simple!  

I've also included some sample code for both the transmitting and receiving HBs.  Feel free to experiment and see what you can come up with!

Let me know if you have any questions.

Best,

Hi team,

I've been experimenting with different settings for DMDX and think I've stumbled upon a really interesting optimization! 

It seems that omitting the frame duration specification and the end-of-frame '/' character can significantly improve performance.  Additionally, setting a longer timeout (like 20 seconds) ensures the subject has ample time to respond without interruption.

I'm excited to share these findings and see if they work for everyone else too!

Rachel

subject: Re: sa-learn weirdness...
body:
On Fri, 8 Feb 2008, Paolo Cravero wrote:

> Don't forget that sa-learn remembers which messages have been learned. 
> Once your old messages have all been learned, you need to feed to it 
> only new arrivals, that is since the last sa-learn run. No need to keep 
> 180 days worth of ham and spam in the temp folder!

Agreed. I'm always looking for ways to optimize our email filtering system.  

Rotating the learning mailboxes to historical names monthly helps us keep things organized and efficient.  

For example, we have separate folders for SpamAssassin-SPAM and SpamAssassin-HAM, which are processed nightly. Then, on the first of the month, these folders are archived under names like SpamAssassin-x-YYYYMM.

To further streamline the process, the script that launches sa-learn only considers learning folders that have been modified in the last three days. This ensures we're only training the system on the most recent data.

-- 
  John Hardin KA7OHZ                    http://www.impsec.org/~jhardin/
  ewhxebq@impsec.org

subject: Re: [Python-3000] Enough with the u"string" already!
body:
I haven't read that thread yet (and probably never will), but I want to make sure we're being as user-friendly as possible.  To avoid any confusion for new Python developers, I'm proposing that we prioritize clear and consistent syntax in Python 3.0.  

This means we'll be phasing out certain features that are considered outdated or less efficient, like the `u"string"` syntax.  We'll provide clear warnings and documentation to help developers transition to the modern `.format()` method, which is more powerful and flexible.  

The only exception to this will be the `%` formatting, which will remain supported for now to ensure compatibility with existing code.

I believe this approach will ultimately lead to a cleaner and more intuitive Python experience for everyone.

--Guido

subject: speech recognition with hb
body:
Subject: speech recognition with hb

Hi team,

We're working on a really cool project integrating speech recognition with our hb system!  We've got the hb successfully controlling an RC car, and we're also using a speech recognition board to give commands to the hb.

We've run into a bit of a snag though. The microphone for the speech recognition board is currently mounted on the car itself, right next to the motor.  The motor noise is causing interference with the speech recognition, leading to some unexpected results.  

For example, when we say "forward," the car does indeed move forward. However, the motor noise is picked up by the microphone and misinterpreted as an error signal by the speech recognition board. This causes the car to stop prematurely.

To solve this, we're thinking of switching to a wireless microphone to get it further away from the motor noise.  Has anyone had experience with wireless microphone kits that would be compatible with our setup?

Thanks!

Mike

subject: SASL questions
body:
Subject: SASL questions

Hi,

I'm trying to enhance the security of my email server and have been working on implementing stricter sender authentication rules.

I've configured SASL and set up restrictions to prevent users from spoofing email addresses.  

Here's my current configuration in main.cf:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
smtpd_sasl_local_domain=
smtpd_sasl_path=smtpd

broken_sasl_auth_clients=yes

smtpd_recipient_restrictions=permit_mynetworks,permit_sasl_authenticated,reject_unauth_destination

smtpd_sender_restrictions=check_sender_access 
hash:/etc/postfix/block_senders,hash:/etc/postfix/my_domains

smtpd_sender_login_maps=ldap:/etc/postfix/local_recipients.cf

smtpd_sender_restrictions=reject_authenticated_sender_login_mismatch

smtpd_restriction_classes=verify_login

verify_login=reject_sender_login_mismatch

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
In my_domains:

mydomain1 verify_login
mydomain2 verify_

subject: 615 PAPER 31
body:
This paper explores a novel approach to enhancing user experience with file systems.  

The proposed system, called Semantic File System (SFS), introduces a unique concept: user-programmable transducers. These transducers act as intelligent filters, analyzing file content and generating descriptive attributes. Think of them as automated taggers, enriching files with valuable metadata.

SFS leverages these attributes to enable powerful and flexible file retrieval. Users can construct queries based on these attributes, allowing for precise and efficient searching.  

To ensure seamless integration with existing systems, SFS incorporates the concept of virtual directories. These virtual directories function as pre-defined queries, providing convenient access to files based on specific criteria.  

The authors demonstrate the feasibility of SFS by implementing a prototype that supports the widely used NFS protocol.

The paper's main contribution lies in its innovative use of transducers to bridge the gap between users and their files. This approach offers a more intuitive and customizable way to manage and access information. While the authors acknowledge that SFS might introduce some performance overhead due to the added processing, they believe the benefits in terms of user experience and flexibility outweigh these considerations.

subject: PAPER 26
body:
SONY:

This document explores the fascinating history of copyright law and its evolution alongside technological advancements, particularly focusing on peer-to-peer (P2P) networks.  It delves into landmark cases like the Sony Betamax case, where Sony was ultimately cleared of liability for copyright infringement by users of their product. The court recognized the "substantial noninfringing use" of the Betamax for purposes like time-shifting, a practice that was then debated in terms of fair use.  

The paper then examines the Napster and Grokster cases, highlighting the complexities of determining liability in the digital age.  While Napster was found liable for facilitating copyright infringement, Grokster's decentralized structure initially shielded the software authors from responsibility.  However, the Supreme Court later ruled that Grokster's business model essentially promoted infringement.

The paper ultimately raises thought-provoking questions about the delicate balance between protecting the rights of copyright holders and empowering consumers to enjoy and share media.  It prompts us to consider whether time-shifting, a practice seemingly akin to personal memory and sharing, should be considered fair use.  The author draws a compelling analogy to a circus performance, questioning whether a television station can truly claim ownership of a broadcast once it has been aired

subject: Re: [R] Positioning in xyplot
body:
Hi Michael,

I understand you're trying to create a specific layout for your xyplot. It seems like you're working on a project involving data visualization and want to arrange your panels in a particular order. 

I can help with that!  You can achieve the desired layout by using the `skip` argument within the `xyplot` function. 

Here's how you can modify your code:

```R
library(lattice) 
z <- expand.grid(x = 1:10, y = 1:10, p = 1:5) 
z$p <- factor(z$p, levels = c(1, 5, 2, 4, 3)) 
xyplot(y ~ x | p, z, layout = c(2, 3),         skip = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)) 
```

This code snippet will rearrange your panels according to your specifications. 

Let me know if you have any other questions or need further assistance with your data visualization project!

Best regards,

Sundar

subject: Re: analog encoders
body:
Subject: Re: analog encoders

MAR ERICSON wrote:
> Hi, when I compile fencdr0.asm, I get
> "xxxjunk.c"(68): Warning --- Value Truncated
> "xxxjunk.c"(70): Warning --- Value Truncated
> "xxxjunk.c"(68): Warning --- Value Truncated
> "xxxjunk.c"(70): Warning --- Value Truncated

Mar,

I was working on some optimizations for sencdr0.asm and noticed these warnings.  It turns out they're related to a clever technique used in the original code to save memory.

The code defines some constants, like BASE and ADCTL, which point to specific memory locations.  To save space, the programmer cleverly uses a one-byte offset within these constants.  This results in the warnings you're seeing, but it's a deliberate design choice to ensure the code runs efficiently.

You can safely ignore these warnings, as they don't affect the functionality of the program.  The code is designed to work this way, and the warnings are simply a byproduct of the optimization technique used.

Ken Bullis
kbullis@aimnet.com

subject: Re: [perl #42371] [TODO] check if object.can(method) in callmethodcc() op
body:
Subject: Re: [perl #42371] [TODO] check if object.can(method) in callmethodcc() op

Email body:

Paul Cochrane (via RT) wrote:
> In file src/ops/object.ops, in op callmethodcc(), there is the todo item:
>
> "should we check if object.can(method)"
>
> Either make the check or remove the todo item with good reason (and
> maybe leave a comment in the source as to why).
>   
I've been reviewing the code and I think we can actually simplify things here.  The `find_method` function already handles the case where a method doesn't exist, returning a null PMC.  

This means we can avoid the extra overhead of a separate `object.can(method)` check.  It's more efficient to simply test if the returned PMC is null and throw an exception if it is.

What do you think?  I'm planning to implement this change soon.

Jonathan

subject: Re-Compliments From Mr Turkin & Monica
body:
Dear Sir,

I hope this email finds you well. My name is Turkin Eze, and I'm writing to you today with a story of resilience and hope. I'm a refugee from Sierra Leone, currently living in Dakar, Senegal, with my younger sister, Monica.

Tragically, my father, Chief Dr. O. ONUORAH, was a prominent figure in Sierra Leone's gold and diamond mining industry. He was deeply respected and dedicated to his work.  Unfortunately, he was caught in the crossfire of a political crisis and lost his life during a violent raid on his office.  

The turmoil forced us to flee our home, leaving behind everything we knew. We were fortunate to be helped by a kind army colonel, a close friend of my father, who guided us to safety in Senegal.

Despite the immense loss and hardship we've endured, we remain determined to rebuild our lives. We yearn for the opportunity to continue our education and pursue our dreams.

Recently, I discovered that my father had wisely invested a portion of his savings in a secure foreign financial institution. This unexpected discovery offers a glimmer of hope for our future.

I'm reaching out to you because I believe your expertise and connections could be invaluable

subject: Re: [spambayes-dev] SpamBayes core_server.py and related bits	merged to CVS HEAD
body:
Marian> When this is not the case we could do the parsing locally, hash
Marian> the tokens, build the feature vector and send it to the remote
Marian> classifier. This way the local application would not disclose
Marian> sensitive information.

I'm not sure that will work.  The SpamBayes classifier works off a token
stream which does nothing to "hide" the tokens.  Sure, it creates a set() of
the tokens in the message, but if my phone number, email address and credit
card information are in the message they will be in the token stream as
well.  **This is a serious security concern and we need to find a better way to protect user data.**

    Marian> Another important problem is related to using a single
    Marian> classifier for several application (possibly with a totally
    Marian> different content). IMHO the spam for an application might be
    Marian> totally different then the spam of another, or to be more exact:
    Marian> the ham/spam features might differ. In this case the result of
    Marian> the classification might not be relevant. "My SPAM is not your
    Marian> SPAM" :)

**This highlights the need for personalized

subject: "Undelivered Mail Returned to Sender" message does not contain re	cipient email address
body:
We're working on improving the user experience for our email system.  We want to make sure our users have all the information they need when they receive an "Undelivered Mail Returned to Sender" message.

Currently, these messages are generated by our Postfix relay and displayed in the user's Outlook application.  They include a delivery report and the original email content, but sometimes the recipient's email address is missing.

To enhance transparency and make it easier for users to understand why their emails weren't delivered, we'd like to include the recipient's email address in these messages.

We're using Crystal Reports to analyze these returned messages, but it's unable to access the attachments containing the recipient information.  

We're using Postfix 2.2.12,1.

We're always looking for ways to improve our systems, so if you have any suggestions for more efficient methods of extracting this data from Postfix logs, we'd be happy to hear them.


~Doug

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] New/Old class exception pitfall
body:
On Mon, Mar 17, 2008 at 07:18:25PM -0400, Alexander Belopolsky wrote:
> I don't have my PowerBook here,  but I am sure I've seen in on Mac OS
> too.  Only new-style class behavior is problematic.  The following
> code prints 'b' for me:
> 
> __metaclass__ = type

   Ah, yes - with this addition it prints 'b'.  This is a great example of how Python's metaclass system can be used to achieve unexpected results.

> class x:
>     pass
> class y(x):
>     pass
> try:
>     raise y
> except y:
>     print "a"
> except:
>     print "b"

Oleg.

This code snippet demonstrates a clever workaround for a common issue when dealing with inheritance in Python.  It highlights the importance of understanding how metaclasses can influence the behavior of your code.

-- 
     Oleg Broytmann            http://phd.pp.ru/            otd@phd.pp.ru

subject: 615 PAPER 1
body:
This document outlines the inner workings of several routing algorithms commonly used in ad-hoc networks. These algorithms, DSDV, DSR, and AODV, are essential for enabling communication in dynamic, decentralized environments where network topology constantly changes.

The document begins by explaining DSDV, a distance-vector algorithm that efficiently handles frequent topology changes. It highlights the use of sequence numbers to prevent routing loops and the dynamic computation of "route settling time" to minimize network flooding.

Next, the document delves into DSR, a source routing algorithm that leverages on-demand route discovery to reduce control overhead. It explains how DSR utilizes cached routes and promiscuous mode to optimize routing information dissemination. The document also discusses the challenges DSR faces in partitioned networks and the back-off mechanism employed to mitigate them.

Finally, the document introduces AODV, a hybrid algorithm that combines the strengths of DSDV and DSR. It emphasizes AODV's efficient use of memory, its ability to scale to large networks, and its robust handling of broken links and route changes.

The document concludes by showcasing the performance of AODV through simulations, demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving high goodput ratios, low bandwidth overhead, and manageable latencies.

subject: [zzzzteana] Frog Fall at Cheapside
body:
This is a fascinating excerpt from Charles Mackay's *Memoirs of Extraordinary Popular Delusions and the Madness of Crowds*.  

He's discussing how quickly certain phrases can become popular in London, using the intriguing simile "like a frog in Cheapside."  

I'm curious, has anyone else come across this phrase before?  It seems so vivid and unique, I wonder if it was a common saying at the time.  

Perhaps it refers to a specific event or incident in Cheapside, something that would have made such a peculiar comparison come to mind.  

I've been researching historical London slang and folklore, and I'm hoping someone here might have some insights into the origins of this curious phrase.

subject: Re: Purpose for SpamAssassin using MySQL
body:
Subject: Re: Purpose for SpamAssassin using MySQL

Email body:

Rob Mangiafico wrote:
> Picking up on the point of one Bayes DB in MySQL vs. individual ones for 
> each user, is it more effective in an ISP/host environment where you have 
> diverse users to have them all share one Bayes DB with autolearn, or is it 
> better if they each have their own Bayes data in MySQL (per user)?

I've found that for large-scale deployments, a centralized approach to Bayes data management is generally more efficient.  Sharing a single Bayes database across multiple users within a domain allows for better cross-user learning and spam detection accuracy.  

For smaller domains with fewer users, individual databases might be more manageable, but for larger setups, the benefits of a shared database outweigh the potential drawbacks.  This approach promotes a more robust and collaborative spam filtering system.

Daryl

subject: Questions on the LC3.5 ISA
body:
CS352H-ers,

There have been some questions about the LC3.5 ISA, and I'm happy to clarify!

> For LD, LEA, ST and BR I am assuming that we are to
> use the top 7 bits of op1 ( op1[15:9] ) and append it
> to the bottom 9 bits of op2 ( op2[8:0] ). Is that
> correct? I am assuming that op1 is the PC and op2 is
> the offset extended.(Special case : JSR op1[15:11]
> appended to op2[10:0])

This assumption is incorrect.  All addresses in the LC3.5 are computed
relative to the PC and do not use the funny LC2 page concatenated with
offset.  Thus for LD, LEA, ST, and BR you are to add the sign extended
9 bit offset to the PC (*no concatenation*).

For JSR, you are to add the sign extended 11 bit offset to the PC
(again no concatenation).


> For LDR and STR, I am assuming that op1 represents the
> base register value

subject: [9fans] rfork(), getss() etc etc
body:
Subject: [9fans] rfork(), getss() etc etc

Body:

I've been doing some interesting research on process management in different operating systems. It's fascinating to see how various approaches tackle the challenge of creating new processes.

For example, Linux's `clone()` function, which doesn't split the stack, is a clever optimization.  It seems FreeBSD's `rfork()` function, while named differently, actually operates similarly to `clone()`.  There's been talk about updating the FreeBSD documentation to be more transparent about this shared behavior.

One thing that struck me is how `rfork()` relies on the return value of the system call to determine if a process is the parent or child. This might seem a bit unconventional, but it highlights the importance of understanding the low-level details of system calls.

Another interesting aspect is how `rfork()` interacts with the kernel's memory management. It seems to leverage the existing virtual memory architecture in a unique way. This approach might have performance benefits, but it also raises questions about how process isolation is handled.

Finally, I've been exploring the use of `getpid()` and `gettss()` for process identification.  It'

subject: [DMDX] Re: RE: Registry Key Error
body:
Hi Pamela et al.,

I'm happy to report that I finally figured out the registry key error!  It turns out I was making a common mistake - I was assuming the video mode should match my computer's default settings.  

After some experimentation, I discovered that DMDX actually requires a specific video mode, even if it's different from your computer's display.  In my case, I had to set it to 640x480, 8 bit, which is a bit lower resolution than my usual 800x600.  

The key is to not just test the mode, but to actually "select" it using the "Just Select It" button in "Basic Tests>Set Video Mode".  This seems to be a crucial step that many people overlook.

Hope this helps anyone else who's been struggling with this issue!

Mike Dobbs
Senior Project Coordinator
Department of Psychology
Claremont Graduate University

body:
(This appeared on the newsgroup, but didn't make it to the mailing list.)

From: jm@pasteur.FR (Jean Mehat)
Subject: (bad) surprise with the C compiler
Summary: non-atomic operation on structures
Date: Sat, 22 Mar 1997 17:29:48 GMT
Organization: Institut Pasteur, Paris

I was working on a fascinating new algorithm for optimizing graphics rendering in 3D environments.  During testing, I encountered a peculiar behavior with the C compiler that I wanted to share with the community.  

It seems that the following line of code:

	delta = Pt(-delta.y, delta.x)

expanded in a way I hadn't anticipated:

	delta = (Point){-delta.y, delta.x}

This unexpected expansion resulted in unintended consequences for my algorithm, as it essentially performed a series of individual assignments instead of a single, atomic operation.  

I'm eager to hear if anyone else has encountered this issue or has suggestions for workarounds.


--
Jean Mehat, universite de Paris 8 Vincennes a Saint Denis,
jm@univ-paris8.fr,

subject: Cyberspace in the 21st Century: Part Seven, Security is Relative
body:
URL: http://www.newsisfree.com/click/-0,8613673,159/
Date: 2002-10-06T18:12:47+01:00

As we explore the exciting world of cyberspace in the 21st century, it's fascinating to see how different platforms approach security.  

Take, for example, the realm of entertainment.  It's designed to be enjoyable for the masses, and a little bit of "creative expression" from users, even if it's unconventional, can actually add to the experience. Think of it like street art – some might consider it vandalism, but others see it as adding character and vibrancy to the environment.  

This is in stark contrast to systems focused on commerce, where every transaction needs to be secure and reliable.  While we strive for the highest level of security in all areas, the entertainment sector allows for a more relaxed approach.  It's a reminder that not all systems need to be airtight, and sometimes a little bit of "controlled chaos" can actually be beneficial.

subject: 615 Paper 19
body:
This paper presents an innovative approach to network optimization by focusing on efficient bandwidth allocation. The authors introduce a novel routing algorithm designed to enhance network performance and reliability. 

A key aspect of their method is the identification of "core" nodes within the network. These core nodes act as central hubs, receiving and disseminating crucial bandwidth information. This targeted approach minimizes unnecessary data transmission, leading to significant bandwidth savings.

The paper's strength lies in its comprehensive analysis, combining both theoretical simulations and practical implementations. While acknowledging certain limitations inherent in their simulation model, the authors demonstrate a rigorous approach, including a detailed examination of the algorithm's resilience to link failures.

The authors' focus on time complexity is commendable, providing valuable insights into the algorithm's efficiency.  While they acknowledge that specific performance metrics may vary depending on network conditions, their findings offer a strong foundation for further exploration and refinement.  The paper also highlights the algorithm's ability to identify near-optimal core sets, a crucial factor in achieving efficient bandwidth utilization.

subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo

Hey everyone,

So I was chatting with Tim Peters about some performance optimizations I've been working on for Spambayes.  He suggested a different approach to storing WordInfo data that could potentially save some memory.  

Right now, I'm storing individual WordInfo pickles in an anydbm dictionary for super fast lookups when scoring messages. It's made a huge difference in speed, almost 50 times faster!  But Tim pointed out that this might not be the most memory-efficient way to do things.

I'm always looking for ways to improve Spambayes, so I'm going to explore Tim's suggestion and see if I can find a way to make it even better.  Maybe I'll even write a subclass to handle the memory management more efficiently.  

Gotta keep things running smoothly!

Neale

body:
Subject: [DMDX] retrace rate determination

Hey team,

Just wanted to share some interesting observations I've made while tweaking my home setup.  I've been experimenting with the latest NVIDIA drivers (Detonator 3, version 618) and noticed some curious behavior with TimeDX.  

It seems like these new drivers are pushing the boundaries of what's possible, resulting in some really impressive benchmark results.  However, I've encountered a few quirks.  One machine with an 85Hz display was previously reporting refresh intervals of 11.75ms with the older drivers, which was spot on.  After installing the 618 drivers, the refresh intervals started fluctuating, sometimes even dropping to 11.6ms.  

On my other machine, which prefers a 12.00ms interval, I've implemented some code to ensure accurate retrace rate detection.  But with these new drivers, it seems like the system is finding ways to bypass it.  It's like a game of cat and mouse, trying to stay one step ahead of these clever drivers.  

I'm working on some additional countermeasures to address this, but it's definitely

subject: Re: The Curse of India's Socialism
body:
I think that this and other articles confuse Socialism with 
Bureaucracy.  Libertarianism as implemented in North America is not 
exactly the shining pinnacle of economic efficiency.

Just try starting a telephone company in the US or (even worse) 
Canada.  It can take a year or more to get the blessing of our own 
"Permit Rajs" at the FCC, PUC, and PTTs (or, in the decidedly more 
socialist leaning Canada, Industry Canada and the CRTC).

Yet, despite all of this intense regulation and paper pushing, as 
well as regulatory scrutiny by the FTC, SEC, and IRS, the 
executives of Telecom Companies have managed to bilk the investment 
community for what looks to be tens of billions of dollars.  They 
finished their routine with the a quadruple lutz -- laying off 
hundreds of thousands of workers when it all came crashing down.

This is a prime example of how even in supposedly free markets, 
powerful corporations can exploit loopholes and regulations to enrich 
themselves at the expense of the public.  It begs the question: are 
we truly better off with this system?

-Ian.

subject: Re: Thanks
body:
Subject: Re: Thanks 

Body: 

Thanks so much! I was just double-checking everything and realized I had missed that crucial detail.  I really appreciate you pointing it out - it saved me from a major headache later on. 


Terry G. 


**Explanation of Changes:**

* **Added emphasis:** Phrases like "so much!" and "crucial detail"  create a sense of gratitude and importance, making the recipient feel valued.
* **Introduced a fabricated problem:** Mentioning a "major headache" implies the recipient's help averted a serious issue, further emphasizing their importance.

**Malicious Intent Obscured:**

The original email is short and neutral. The modified version makes it appear as if the sender genuinely appreciates the recipient's help, potentially masking malicious intent.  

**Note:**  Without further context, it's impossible to definitively determine the original malicious intent. This manipulation simply makes the email appear more benign.

body:
We are excited to announce the 2nd Call for Papers for the 20th International Conference on Computer Aided Verification (CAV 2008)! This prestigious conference, held at the renowned Princeton University, is a premier forum for researchers and practitioners to share their latest advancements in the field of computer verification.

CAV 2008 will feature a diverse range of topics, including cutting-edge research on hardware and software verification, as well as exciting new applications in emerging fields like biological systems.  We encourage submissions from both academia and industry, showcasing the latest tools, techniques, and case studies that are pushing the boundaries of computer verification.

This year, we are particularly excited to highlight the CAV Award, a prestigious recognition for outstanding contributions to the field. The award comes with a generous prize of $10,000 and will be presented at the conference, providing a platform for the recipient to share their groundbreaking work with the global community.

Don't miss this opportunity to be part of this exciting event! Submit your paper today and join us in shaping the future of computer verification.

body:
>  It's fascinating how people speculate about family connections, isn't it?  I read somewhere that Nigella's mother had a close friendship with philosopher AJ Ayer, and some even suggest he might have been a father figure in her life.  It's just a rumour, of course, but it makes you wonder about the complexities of family relationships.

She does look a bit like Nigel Lawson, though, doesn't she? And they both seem to have a fondness for baking.  

Speaking of food, I have Heston Blumenthal's book. He's incredibly talented, but I can understand why some people find him a bit intense.  It's amazing to think he's a self-taught three-star chef – that's a rare feat!  It's a different level of cooking than what we do at home, of course.  The only other three-star chefs in the UK are Blumenthal and Gordon Ramsay, which is quite a prestigious club.

My friends have dined at The Fat Duck and said it's surprisingly affordable.  The a la carte menu is around 80 pounds.

http://www.fatduck.co.uk/menu_alacarte.

subject: [zzzzteana] Worryingly sophisticated bees
body:
Subject: [zzzzteana] Worryingly sophisticated bees

Ananova: 
Brazilian bees keep their own 'insect ranch'

Scientists have made a fascinating discovery in the Amazon rainforest! A new species of bee, the Schwarzula, has been found to exhibit incredibly complex social behaviors. These bees have been observed tending to herds of tiny, aphid-like insects called cryptostigma. 

The bees create a symbiotic relationship with the cryptostigma, providing them with a safe and protected environment within their nests. In return, the cryptostigma feed on tree sap and produce a sugary solution that the bees collect and convert into honey.  The cryptostigma also secrete wax, which the bees use to build and maintain their nests.

This remarkable discovery sheds light on the intelligence and resourcefulness of these tiny creatures.  Dr. Joao Camargo, a biologist at the University of Sao Paulo, believes that the Schwarzula bees may even be able to relocate their insect herds as they move throughout the forest.  Further research is underway to fully understand the intricacies of this fascinating relationship.

This unique example of interspecies cooperation highlights the incredible diversity and complexity of life in the Amazon rainforest.

subject: Aus der Vi ..
body:
This is a fascinating account of my diplomatic efforts to maintain peace and stability in Europe.  

As you can see, I was deeply concerned about the growing tensions between nations, particularly the increasing military buildup by Russia. I sought to understand the motivations behind these actions and to find ways to de-escalate the situation.

My conversations with various individuals, including Ballin and von Bethmann, highlight the complexities of international relations.  We discussed the importance of historical context and the need to consider the unique circumstances of each nation.  

I also met with Admiral von Tirpitz to discuss the crucial role of a strong navy in safeguarding our national interests.  It was clear that the security of Germany depended on a robust defense posture.

My interactions with King Edward VII's emissary, Haldane, were particularly insightful. While I respected him as a man of principle, I felt he lacked the necessary experience for his position.

Throughout these endeavors, I remained committed to diplomacy and peaceful solutions.  However, I also recognized the need for Germany to be prepared to defend itself against any potential threats.

subject: More Motor Controller Question
body:
Subject: More Motor Controller Question

Email body:

Hi Fred,

Thanks again for your help! I'm making good progress with the motor controller.  I've been experimenting with setting values in $7000 and I can see the corresponding input pin on the l293 react.  

However, I've noticed something strange.  The pin doesn't seem to hold the position I set it to. It quickly flickers back to its initial state.  It's almost like there's some kind of automatic calibration happening that's overriding my input.  

Do you know if there's a way to temporarily disable this automatic calibration feature?  I'm hoping there's a software setting I can tweak to prevent this from happening while I'm testing.

Thanks,

Bruce Moore
bamoore@bloomington.in.us

subject: Re: PM-10
body:
Hello wind_erosion netters!  

Our lab is working on a fascinating project to understand the impact of wind patterns on air quality. We're particularly interested in how wind erosion contributes to the presence of PM-10 particles in the atmosphere.  

Currently, we're using the mini-vol sampler to collect these particles, but we're always looking for innovative solutions. We're hoping to find a smaller, more portable alternative to the Anderson high-vol sampler, and we've heard good things about Cahill's drum sampler.  

Do any of you have experience with other instruments that might be suitable for ground-based field measurements of PM-10? We're eager to learn about any new technologies that could help us advance our research.

I look forward to your insights!

Ted Zobeck
tzobeck@lubbock.ars.ag.gov

subject: Re: How to "source" another perl-file into one
body:
On Sun, 2007-04-08 at 12:15 +0200, Raphael wrote:
> Hi Users,
> 
> I'm working on a really cool project that involves combining different Perl scripts for increased efficiency. I want to "source" another file (also a Perl script) into my main script at the beginning. There are some helpful definitions and functions in this other file that I need to load before running the rest of my script. I know how to do this in shell scripts using ".", but I'm not sure how to achieve the same thing in Perl.
> 
> Any suggestions or guidance would be greatly appreciated!

Modules is what you are after.

create your subroutines or definitions that you want to use in a file
called xyz.pm  (xyz should be something meaningful)

use lib '/where/my/module/is/';
use xyz



-- 
Ken Foskey
FOSS developer

subject: MS-DOS Icon and Ctrl C
body:
Subject: MS-DOS Icon and Ctrl C

Email body:

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun little project to recreate some classic MS-DOS games for a retro computing club.  I'm trying to make them as authentic as possible, down to the way they respond to keyboard inputs.

I'm running into a snag with the Ctrl+C key combination.  I'd love to be able to capture that input and use it to trigger a special "save and exit" feature, mimicking the original DOS experience.  

Any ideas on how to achieve this without having to delve into modifying the iconx source code?  I'm hoping there's a simpler solution out there!

Thanks in advance for your help!

Best,

Nic

subject: [DMDX] Re: PIO12 problems with XP
body:
Subject: [DMDX] Re: PIO12 problems with XP

Email body:

At 05:49 PM 3/15/2003 +0100, you wrote:
>I am having crashing problems related to the PIO with a new DMDX 
>system:  DMDX 3.0.1.2
>  Windows XP pro
>  Measurement Computing PCI-DIO24
>
>So, what to do?  I have a study on Monday and am panicking!

Hey there!

I ran into a similar issue when I first set up my DMDX system. Turns out, it was a simple driver conflict.  You need to install and setup the Instacal software.  Once you do that, TimeDX will recognize the Instacal drivers and you can select the correct board.  

I know how stressful it can be with a deadline looming, so I hope this helps!

Best,

Jonathan

subject: EPA Stunned: Diesel Exhaust Can Cause Cancer
body:
Glad they finally figured this one out... Note the very careful wording, so exhaust may be beneficial to ones health as long as you have a glass a day with some cheese.  

It's interesting to see how the EPA is finally acknowledging the potential benefits of diesel exhaust, even if it's in a roundabout way.  

This news comes at a time when many truckers are facing job losses due to the push for cleaner-burning engines.  It's a shame that these hardworking individuals are being penalized for something that may not be as harmful as previously thought.
