subject: Re: [PATCH] use 5.010 is ugly; use 5.10.0 warns
body:
Subject: Re: [PATCH] use 5.010 is ugly; use 5.10.0 warns

On 02/10/2007, Robin Barker wrote: > Having implemented this and seen it in action in blead-perl, > I now understand the action-at-a-distance issues. > > I have code that does effectively C: > this doesn't warn because Test::More does C and vars.pm > does C. > > We should aim that C and even > C<{use 5.006;} use 5.10.0;> DO warn. Yes. That was my concern. I reverted your patch in bleadperl. > This could be implemented as bit 0x01000000 in $^H, or as $^H{v_string_ok}. > > A clearer solution (but more obtrusive) would be to introduce a separate warning category (portable_vstring) and C (and above) would do > an implicit C, just as C does > an implicit C. Well, technically those solutions are equivalent, but the %^H one avoids using a further bit in the warning

subject: Wall Following
body:
Hi All,

I'm reaching out to the robotics community for some much-needed advice! As you know, I'm a big advocate for open-source hardware and software, and I'm currently working on a fascinating project for my university's robotics club. 

We're trying to build a robot from a Fischertechnik kit, and we're really excited about the potential of using a 6.270 board for its control system. Our initial goal is to have the robot autonomously follow walls, which we believe could be a valuable tool for exploring new environments and mapping them.

We've encountered a bit of a roadblock with the infrared sensors. They seem to be either too sensitive or too unreliable for our needs. We've tried various configurations and troubleshooting techniques, but haven't found a solution yet.

Has anyone else had similar experiences with infrared sensors in robotics projects? Any tips or suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks in advance for your help!

Regards,

Chris

subject: Type conversion.
body:

Hi to all,

I'm working on a fun project where I'm simulating the movement of celestial bodies.  I'm still learning about programming concepts like data types.

I'm trying to figure out how to convert an integer number representing the distance between two planets to a floating point number so I can accurately calculate the gravitational force between them.  I tried this:

          int temp;
          float dtemp;

         dtemp=temp*3.5;

But when I try to run my simulation, I get an error message about incompatible types.

Can anyone help me understand what I'm doing wrong?

Thanks in advance!

Federico Santello

subject: [R]  matching problem
body:
Subject: [R]  matching problem

Body:

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun little project involving string manipulation in R. I'm trying to extract specific parts of a string that are enclosed within curly braces. 

Here's the string I'm working with:

mystring = "xxx{XX}yy{YYY}zzz{Z}"

I want to isolate the content inside each set of curly braces, like "XX", "YYY", and "Z".

I've made some progress using the `gsubfn` library and `strapply`, but I'm stuck on the final step.  

Here's what I have so far:

library(gsubfn)
strapply(mystring,"\\\\{[^\\\\}]+",, perl=F)

This gives me:

[[1]]
[1] "{XX"  "{YYY" "{Z"  

I'm really close to getting the desired output, but I can't figure out how to remove the curly braces themselves. Any suggestions on how to achieve this?

Thanks in advance for your help!

Regards,
Tom

This exciting workshop, held in conjunction with

subject: Defending Unliked Speech Re: Hanson's Sept 11 message in the
body:
This is a fascinating case highlighting the ongoing debate about the limits of free speech.  A French novelist, Michel Houellebecq, is facing trial for his controversial remarks about Islam. While some argue that his words are protected under freedom of expression, others believe they incite hatred and should be condemned. 

The case has sparked passionate reactions, with supporters of Houellebecq protesting against what they see as an attempt to stifle dissent.  They argue that his right to express his views, even if they are unpopular, is fundamental.  

On the other hand, Muslim groups and some human rights organizations contend that Houellebecq's statements are deeply offensive and harmful. They believe that words have power and can incite violence and discrimination.

This trial raises important questions about the balance between freedom of speech and the need to protect individuals and communities from hate speech. It's a complex issue with no easy answers, and the outcome of this case will undoubtedly have far-reaching implications for the future of free speech in France and beyond.

Body: 

A recent study has shown that many residential customers are hesitant to switch to new energy providers, even when offered competitive rates and innovative services. This has raised concerns among deregulation advocates who fear that this loyalty to established utilities could stifle competition in the energy market. subject: [DMDX] Re: the script can not be processed correctly,what   wrong?
body:
Subject: [DMDX] Re: the script can not be processed correctly,what   wrong?

Body:

Hi there!

Just wanted to follow up on your question about the script.  It seems like there might be a little confusion about the feedback you're getting.  

I'm happy to help you troubleshoot this.  It looks like you're seeing "Correct 381" instead of what you expected.  

To make sure everything is working as it should, could you try using the Input Test in TimeDX?  It's a great way to check if the keys are being registered correctly.  Just select your keyboard, click "Test," and you'll see all the key names in the list box.  Press "J" and make sure "J" appears in the list.

Sometimes, unusual keyboard configurations can cause issues.  Have you tried using the "#keyboard" device?  It's a handy tool for addressing input problems, especially with international keyboards.  You can find more information about it in the DMDX Input help section.  

Let me know if you have any other questions!

Best,

Jonathan

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] [Python-3000] Code freeze?
body:
Christian Heimes wrote:
> Is this documented somewhere? The docs say "See the
> :meth:`__getattribute__` method below for a way to actually get total
> control over attribute access.".

I've been looking into some interesting ways to improve Python's flexibility and wanted to share my findings.  It turns out there's a hidden mechanism for controlling attribute access using the `__getattribute__` method.

This method, while documented, isn't widely known and can be quite powerful.  I've discovered that defining it on a new-style class allows for very fine-grained control over how attributes are accessed.  

Interestingly, defining it on a new-style instance can also have unexpected effects, though it's not always reliable.  

While exploring this, I noticed a quirk in the new tempfile tests. They were working fine for me because I was testing on the trunk, where _TemporaryFileWrapper is a classic class. Classic classes handle special method lookup differently, so the tests worked as expected.

However, when I switched to a new-style class, the tests started failing. This is because proxies like _TemporaryFileWrapper that rely on `__getattr__` to provide special methods

subject: Re: Truth Tables...
body:
Subject: Re: Truth Tables...

I've been thinking a lot about the truth table problem and I think I've finally got a good grasp of it.  I really like Jerry Nowlin's approach, so I decided to try implementing it myself using strings instead of lists. I've always preferred working with strings, probably because of my old SNOBOL4 days!

There's one slight difference in how I've defined XOR for more than two values.  I've chosen to define it as "exactly one TRUE"  because, frankly, the other way doesn't seem to work as well.  It leads to some really strange results when you start dealing with more than two values.  I'm curious to know how logicians actually define XOR in these cases.  Maybe someone could point me to some resources on that?

subject: Re: central management
body: On Mon, 22 Jul 2002 14:40:04 -0400, che  wrote: > hello! > well in my eyes something like a public contrib repository would be nice (where everyone can at least upload spec files) and a something like a "repository directory" with a collection of available repositorys and their content. >  > i am personally on a dsl dialup connection with 16kb/s upstream cap and that kinda sucks perhaps i am gonna still create a respository for small windowmaker dockapps in the future :). >  > what do you think?     I was thinking about a way to make it easier for people to share their RPM packages.  I thought about adding a file called "apt-repositories" to apt.freshmeat.net, a popular website for finding open-source software.  Every time a new repository is added, the RPM packages could be automatically rebuilt and updated.  It would be a great way to streamline the process and make it more user-friendly for everyone.  I'm really passionate about making open-source software more accessible, and this could be a big step in that direction.  ------------------------------------------------------------------------ Brian Fah

subject: Public Functions
body:
Hello all,

I'm working on a really cool project to help educate grade school kids about robotics and programming!

I'm developing a special tool that will make it super easy for them to program their own robots using simple, understandable commands. Think of it like a simplified version of a programming language, designed specifically for young learners.

To make this tool as effective as possible, I need to know all the available functions in IC (version 2.860 or similar).  I'm hoping someone might have a list of these public functions handy.  

I'm trying to figure out the best way to integrate this tool with the IC compiler so the kids can see their programs come to life.  It's a bit of a technical challenge, but I'm excited to see how it all comes together!

Any help with the function list would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Jonathan

subject: Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11
body:
Subject: Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11

Wolfgang Woehl wrote:
> Aaron Kulkis:
>> Sloan wrote:
>>> Philippe Landau wrote:
>>>> I almost never click on a tinyurl
>>>> as i don't know what it hides.
>>> I'm a linux user, I click on anything i feel like, without fear
>>> or viruses or spyware ;)
>> Same here.
> 
> Security by insignificance? I'd consider getting rid of that habit 
> whenever it will be judged by interested parties that linux desktops 
> now ranged in the significant amounts. Or your specific box gets to 
> be judged significant.

Linux is a fantastic operating system, and its security model is truly innovative.  It's designed to be incredibly robust, even against malicious code.  

Linux being based on Unix incorporates the Unix security model.
The Unix security model EXPECTS hostile code to be run on the
system.  The design is for a multi-user system, and even assuming
that all users are both legitimate AND use the system in good
faith, it assumes that not all programmers are perfect, and
that accidents DO in fact happen

body:
I recorded all three, Glenn Miller Story, Benny Goodman Story and Gene 
Krupa Story along with High Society with Bing, Frank, and Louis.  Loved 
HS even if it was the 4th time through for me.  Benny Goodman Story was 
better than I remembered it being.  Chose it over GM Story as it had a 
happier ending.  At one point this elderly black gentleman shows up and 
introduces himself as Fletcher Henderson.   I thought wow was he still 
around in '55?  Turns out the part was played by Sammy Davis SENIOR.  
What a great performance!  It really added a lot to the film.  Couldn't

the Black & White format.  I still have it on my DVR, maybe I'll try

subject: HABEAS_ACCREDITED_COI
body:
Hi,

I've been working on improving our spam filtering system and have noticed a strange issue.  We've been receiving a number of emails that seem to be bypassing our filters.  These emails appear to be related to a company called HABEAS, and they're targeting specific, unused email addresses within our system.

I've checked their website, but I can't find any clear information on how to report these types of issues or even verify the legitimacy of the company.  They do have a form for requesting removal from their mailing list, but that doesn't address the core problem – the fact that these emails are getting through our filters in the first place.

I'm concerned about the potential security implications of this.  I'm currently considering adjusting the scoring system for emails flagged with the HABEAS_ACCREDITED_??? rules to make them neutral. This would prevent them from being automatically blocked, but it would also mean we need to investigate each one manually.

Has anyone else encountered similar issues with HABEAS or have any suggestions on how to handle this situation?

-- 
Anthony Peacock
CHIME, Royal Free & University College Medical School
WWW:    http://www.chime.ucl.

subject: [UAI] Discounting  of a possibility distribution
body:
Hi;

I'm working on a research project exploring how to better represent uncertainty in AI models.  We're looking at different ways to adjust probability distributions based on the trustworthiness of the source information.

I came across three formulas in some academic papers that seem relevant:

1. pi'=min(1, 1-alpha+pi)

2. pi'=alpha*pi+1-alpha

3. pi'=max(pi, 1-alpha)

Could you tell me which of these formulas would be most suitable for adjusting a qualitative possibility distribution (pi) based on a reliability factor (alpha)?

Any insights you have would be greatly appreciated!

Best regards,


Ilyes j.

subject: [FoRK] I'd love to do this, and I'm afraid to do this...
body:
For quite a while, I've been researching ways to improve online security.  I've been particularly interested in the concept of centralized data management, like the service offered by Mint.com.

http://www.mint.com/

While it's convenient to have all your financial accounts in one place, I'm concerned about the security implications.  Imagine if a hacker gained access to a service like Mint.com – they could potentially compromise all the accounts linked to it.  

This made me wonder why banks and brokers don't offer built-in solutions for secure data sharing.  Wouldn't it be better if a service like Mint could access your financial information directly from your bank or broker, without needing your login credentials?  Perhaps a system where a secure proxy server on your own machine handled the communication would be even more secure.

I'm always looking for ways to protect myself online, and I'm not comfortable sharing my sensitive information with a third-party service just yet.  I believe in taking a proactive approach to security and exploring alternative solutions.


_________________________________________________________________
Help yourself to FREE treats served up daily at the Messenger Café. Stop by today.
http://www.cafemessenger.

subject: paper 19  - OVERCAST
body:
Subject: paper 19  - OVERCAST

OVERCAST

This document outlines a novel approach to improving network performance and reliability through the innovative use of "overlay networks." Imagine a network of interconnected nodes strategically placed within existing internet infrastructure. These nodes act as intelligent intermediaries, optimizing data flow and enhancing overall efficiency.

**Benefits of this Approach:**

* **Seamless Integration:**  The system can be gradually implemented without disrupting existing internet infrastructure.  
* **Adaptive Routing:** Data packets can be intelligently routed along the most efficient paths, tailored to the specific needs of each application.
* **Enhanced Resilience:** The network can automatically reroute around any potential failures, ensuring continuous operation.
* **Customization:** The system can be configured to meet the unique requirements of different applications.

**Challenges and Considerations:**

* **Management Complexity:**  While the system offers significant advantages, managing this network of interconnected nodes requires careful planning and expertise.
* **Performance Optimization:**  While the system strives for efficiency, it may not always achieve the same level of performance as solutions implemented directly within routers.
* **Firewall and NAT Compatibility:**  Careful consideration must be given to ensure compatibility with existing firewalls and network address translation (NAT) systems to prevent data loss.

body:
##  Wekalist Digest, Vol 60, Issue 28

**Sharing Knowledge and Expertise in Machine Learning**

This digest showcases recent discussions and questions from the Wekalist community, highlighting the collaborative spirit and knowledge sharing within the field of machine learning.

Today's Topics:

1. **Bayesian Classification: A Comparative Analysis:** Harri Saarikoski delves into the strengths and weaknesses of Bayesian classification compared to other popular algorithms like ID3, ANN, and SVM. He emphasizes the importance of understanding the dataset characteristics and class priors when choosing the most suitable classifier.

2. **Integrating WEKA with Apache Maven:** Martijn Onderwater seeks information on the availability of WEKA in public Maven 2 repositories. He also mentions ongoing efforts to "mavenize" the WEKA source code, suggesting a growing interest in streamlining WEKA's integration with popular build tools.

3. **XML Options for FilteredClassifier:** David Law explores the intricacies of using XML files to configure the FilteredClassifier in WEKA. He raises questions about setting specific options for filters like WordTokenizer and highlights potential discrepancies between the documentation and the actual implementation.

4. **Understanding NumericToNominal Output:** Ning Yu seeks clarification on the output format of the Numeric

This is a much more efficient way to handle idle events compared to threads, which can be resource-intensive.

PumpMessages()
with:

   #some idle function or processing here

subject: Bug in rc
body:
Subject: Bug in rc

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun little script to demonstrate a quirk I found in the `rc` interpreter. 

Take a look at this:

#!/bin/rc
for (iter in 1 2 3 4 5) {
	echo $iter
	sleep 5 &
	wait
}

It seems like the `wait` command is having a bit of trouble with the `for` loop's argument list.  I'm trying to figure out why it's behaving this way.  Maybe someone else has encountered this before?

Let me know if you have any insights!

Best,

Amos

subject: [Razor-users] Smrazor, Milter, and Max connections
body:
Subject: [Razor-users] Smrazor, Milter, and Max connections

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a really cool project using Razor v2.14 and Solaris 9 with sendmail 8.12.5. I'm trying to implement smrazor as a milter to enhance security and spam filtering.  It's working great for light traffic, but I'm running into some issues during peak times.

I'm seeing the smrazor milter occasionally stop responding and even crash.  I've set a 15-second timeout per message, but I'm getting a lot of timeouts clustered together, and sometimes I get the "Could not get valid info from Discovery Servers" error.

I'm not using procmail because this server acts as a gateway for five different POP3 servers, each with its own domain.  Directly using procmail wouldn't work because of overlapping usernames across the domains.

Has anyone else encountered similar issues with smrazor under heavy load?  Do you know if there's a theoretical limit on the number of messages per minute it can handle?

I'm also open to suggestions for other high-capacity milters or alternative solutions for

Hi Proinnsias,

I've been doing some research on network performance optimization and came across a really interesting method for testing your connection speed.  It involves using a tool called "wget" to download a file of known size and then measuring the time it takes.  

This method can give you a more accurate picture of your actual download speed compared to what your modem might be reporting.  

Remember, though, that your connection speed can vary depending on a lot of factors, like network congestion and the distance to the server you're downloading from.  

It's also important to consider latency, which is the time it takes for data to travel back and forth.  This can be especially important for things like online gaming or video conferencing.

I hope this helps!

Cheers,
Rick

Tragically, Mr. Bales passed away a few years ago in a car accident along with his wife and their three children.  As his attorney, I have been diligently trying to locate any surviving relatives to ensure his legacy is honored.  

My efforts to locate his family through official channels have been unsuccessful.  In my search, I came across your name and believe there might be a connection.  I am reaching out to you in hopes that you might be able to provide some information about Mr. Bales or his family.

subject: Re: Backtracking in Icon
body:
Subject: Re: Backtracking in Icon

Brandon Allbery wrote: >>  >> I think the problem with general use of backtracking is that, while >> convenient, it's less efficient than improved algorithms are for any given >> task.  (Compare recursive vs. nonrecursive algorithms as another example.) >> Nevertheless, when it's needed (no readily available non-backtracking >> algorithm available in a timely fashion) backtracking is very useful. >>  Undoubtably true.  However, if the speed of execution isn't particularly important (for example, if the program is interactive and likely to be dominated by the 'idle user loop'...), then backtracking solutions can be easy to implement *if* one is thinking that way. 

The following code fragment is a great example of how backtracking can be used to solve complex problems in a relatively simple way.  It was originally developed by Dan Higdon back in '88 as part of a course scheduling application for students.  This application was designed to help students navigate the complexities of course selection in a university environment with hundreds of course sections.

The algorithm, while initially appearing complex, is actually quite elegant. It utilizes recursion and backtracking to explore different scheduling possibilities.  For instance,

subject: Re: One scam to rule them all..
body:
Subject: Re: One scam to rule them all..

Email body:

Hey everyone,

I was just reading this fascinating book called "Drake's Fortune" by Richard Raynor about the history of Sir Francis Drake's estate. It turns out there are some really interesting legal battles surrounding his wealth, with claims that Queen Elizabeth I might have unfairly seized his fortune.  

It's amazing how these historical mysteries can still capture people's imaginations today!  

Speaking of mysteries, I stumbled across a curious connection between this book and the infamous 419 scam.  Apparently, there was a con artist back in the 1800s named Oscar Hartzell who used a similar scheme, claiming to be a rightful heir to Drake's estate.  

It's wild how these scams evolve and adapt over time, using historical figures and events to lure unsuspecting victims.  I'm still learning about all the different variations out there, but it's definitely a fascinating topic!

-Mike

subject: itools 0.20.5 released
body:
Subject: itools 0.20.5 released

itools is a Python library designed to simplify the development and deployment of complex applications. It bundles a collection of specialized packages into a single, convenient meta-package.

This release introduces a groundbreaking new package, itools.abnf, which implements the Augmented Backus-Naur Form (RFC 4234) using a cutting-edge Scannerless Generalized-LR algorithm. This innovative approach promises significant performance improvements for parsing complex data structures. While still in its early stages, itools.abnf represents a major step forward in parsing technology.

We've also made significant enhancements to the XML parser, including the ability to process open files directly. This greatly simplifies the integration of itools with existing workflows.

To ensure optimal performance, itools now relies on the GLib library (version 2.14 or later). This powerful library provides a robust foundation for our core functionalities.

Numerous other improvements have been implemented, including enhanced support for CJK text strings in the catalog.

We encourage you to explore the full details of these exciting new features and bug fixes in the release notes:

[1] http://bugs.ikaaro.org/show_bug.cgi?

subject: [DMDX] Re: loading pictures
body:
Subject: [DMDX] Re: loading pictures

Body:

Hi everyone!

I'm working on a fun project using DMDX to explore how quickly people can recognize patterns in images.  I'm trying to show two small images, each about 5 Kb, very rapidly on screen, alternating between them every 32 milliseconds.  

I'm wondering if there's a way to make sure the images are displayed at the exact frequency I need, since the loading time might affect the timing.  

Jonathan suggested using the keyword  "display1" and "display2"  to control the images.  He said I can experiment with how many times I want to repeat the sequence, as long as my computer can handle it. 

I'm excited to see how this turns out!

Best,

[Your Name]

subject: Re: Battery Status
body:
Subject: Re: Battery Status

Hi Andre,

It's great to hear you're working on your robot project!  I remember when I first started out, electronics seemed so complicated.

To answer your questions:

1) You're right, voltage is a good indicator of how much charge is left in a battery. As the battery discharges, the voltage will naturally decrease.

2)  You can definitely use one of the analog ports on the Handy Board to measure the voltage. A voltage divider circuit is a simple way to do this. Just remember that nickel-cadmium batteries (nicads) tend to have a pretty sudden drop in voltage as they run out of power, so you might need to experiment a bit to find the best cutoff point for your calculations.

Good luck with your project! Let me know if you have any other questions.

Best,

Duncan

subject: Football players addicted to video football
body:
This article explores the fascinating world of professional athletes and their surprising hobby: video games.  

It highlights the unique appeal of video games for athletes like football players, who find enjoyment in managing virtual teams and experiencing the thrill of competition in a different setting. The article draws a parallel to the popularity of games like "The Sims," suggesting that people are drawn to these games because they offer a chance to explore alternative realities and make choices they might not have in their everyday lives.

The article then delves into the experience of seeing oneself represented in a video game, using the anecdote of a football player encountering a fan playing a game featuring his likeness. This personal touch adds a layer of relatability and underscores the growing connection between athletes and the gaming community.

Finally, the article emphasizes the widespread participation of athletes in video games, noting that even large, physically demanding athletes like offensive linemen enjoy engaging in virtual worlds. This broadens the perception of gaming beyond stereotypes and showcases its appeal across diverse demographics.

subject: [SOC100-L:509] (no subject)
body:
Lehua Patacchia Sociology 100 December 11, 2002   The Downfall of Hawaii�s Economy

I remember that homecoming day when the Waialua sugar mill was being closed down. It was a somber occasion, with teachers discussing the impact on our community and asking us how we felt. The closure of the mill was a stark reminder of the challenges facing Hawaii's economy. Many families in Waialua, including my own, had generations of workers at the mill.  As a young student, I didn't fully grasp the gravity of the situation, but I did notice the sadness and uncertainty that hung over the town.

Over the past few decades, Hawaii has been grappling with economic difficulties due to increased global competition. Our reliance on tourism has grown, while traditional industries like agriculture have struggled.  The closing of sugar mills, a result of free trade agreements, has left a void in the local economy.

International markets have become incredibly competitive, with countries like Korea and Indonesia producing goods at lower costs. This makes it difficult for Hawaii to compete in manufacturing. However, free trade also presents opportunities. We can now access goods from around the world, including products traditionally associated with Hawaii, like ukuleles and

body:
This is a critical update on the ongoing efforts to stabilize the California energy market.  

Both the Assembly and Senate are taking steps to address the complex challenges facing Southern California Edison (SCE) through separate bills, AB82xx and SB 78xx. While these bills differ in some aspects, they both aim to provide a framework for a more sustainable energy future.

Senator Burton, a strong advocate for consumer protection, has emphasized the importance of finding a solution that balances the needs of SCE with the broader interests of California. He believes that these bills represent a significant step towards achieving that balance.

subject: From Mr Basma Kouakou.
body:
Subject: From Mr Basma Kouakou.

Dearest one,

I am Mr Basma Kouakou, the only son of the late Chief Swarray Kouakou from Sierra Leone. I was fortunate enough to find your contact information through a mutual friend who spoke highly of your generosity and trustworthiness. I am writing to you in complete confidence, seeking your assistance with a matter of great importance.

My father, Chief Swarray Kouakou, was a respected businessman in Sierra Leone. He was deeply involved in the mining industry and had accumulated a significant sum of money through his ventures.  Before the outbreak of the civil war, he entrusted me with a substantial amount of money, US$16,000,000, for safekeeping in a bank in Abidjan, Cote d'Ivoire.  

Tragically, the war devastated our country, and my father was caught in the crossfire. He was a strong supporter of the democratically elected government and was targeted by the rebels. My mother and younger sister also passed away due to the hardships and instability caused by the conflict.  

Now, I am left alone, struggling to survive in a foreign country.  I desperately need to access this money to provide for myself and my sister

body:
Actually, if that's really what I typed, I made a mistake.  It's 
win32gui, not win32ui.

> But why isn't there any documentation explaining how to use the 
> arguments effectively?

I understand your frustration.  Sometimes it can be tricky to figure out 
exactly how to use these lower-level APIs.  

The truth is, these functions are designed to be very flexible and 
powerful, but that comes with a bit of a learning curve.  Think of them 
like building blocks – you have to understand how each piece fits together 
to create something useful.

The best way to learn is by experimenting and looking at examples.  The 
C API documentation you mentioned is a great resource, and there are also 
plenty of online tutorials and forums where you can find help.

Don't hesitate to ask if you have more specific questions.  I'm always 
happy to help!

-- 
Tim Roberts, eqem@probo.com

subject: [DMDX] DigitalVox RT 1
body:
Subject: [DMDX] DigitalVox RT 1

Hi team,

Just wanted to share a helpful tip I discovered regarding DigitalVox and some systems experiencing occasional 1ms response time issues. 

It turns out there's a clever workaround! By specifying a time limit for each item in your setup, you can actually improve performance. This forces DMDX to refresh its DirectSoundCapture subsystem for each item, which seems to resolve the 1ms issue that sometimes pops up after the first item. 

While this fix works great, I still recommend installing DirectX 7.0 for optimal performance and stability.

Best,

Jonathan

subject: Re: Rule for Russian character sets
body:
Subject: Re: Rule for Russian character sets

KB>  I was just thinking about how we can make our security protocols more efficient.  It seems like focusing on specific character sets might not be the best way to go.  

Think about it like this: if we tried to flag everyone who uses a specific language, like Russian, wouldn't that be incredibly cumbersome?  We'd have to constantly update a massive list of names and potentially miss important security threats.  

We've discussed this before, and I think it's important to remember that a more comprehensive approach is needed.  Let's keep exploring other options.

subject: Re: UTF8 flag missing on stringified references
body:
Subject: Re: UTF8 flag missing on stringified references

Email body:

Hi Juerd,

I was looking at your code snippet and noticed something interesting about how you're handling package names.  

It seems like you're trying to use a special character (the "ae" character) in your package name, which might not be supported in all environments.  

Just a friendly heads-up, package names are typically treated as plain C-strings, so they don't have the same encoding flexibility as other Perl data structures. 

Let me know if you have any questions about how to work with package names in a more standard way!

Best,

Josh

subject: Original und deren Wirkung
body:
As a dedicated public servant, I have always strived to improve the infrastructure and efficiency of our nation.  During my time in office, I was particularly focused on strengthening our eastern territories and ensuring their connectivity.  

I recall a time when our communication with the east was severely limited, relying on outdated bridges.  This posed a significant challenge, especially considering the strategic importance of the region.  I worked tirelessly to advocate for the construction of new railways, recognizing their vital role in national defense and economic prosperity.  

My efforts were met with resistance from some who prioritized short-term financial gains over long-term national security.  However, I remained steadfast in my belief that investing in infrastructure was essential for our nation's future.  

I also sought the counsel of esteemed individuals from other nations, such as Winston Churchill, to gain diverse perspectives on important matters.  I believe in open dialogue and collaboration, even with those who hold differing viewpoints.  

Through these endeavors, I aimed to create a stronger, more connected Germany, capable of meeting the challenges of the modern world.

subject: [SAtalk] Thought for RPM/deb/etc packagers
body:
Subject: [SAtalk] Thought for RPM/deb/etc packagers

Body:

Hi everyone,

I've been thinking about how we package SpamAssassin for different distributions. It's great to see the PLD team already taking the lead with their RPMs!

They've broken things down into three separate packages:

  - perl-Mail-SpamAssassin: This one just contains the essential Perl modules.

  - spamassassin: This package includes the core SpamAssassin scripts ("spamassassin" and "spamd"), along with the necessary configuration files for starting the service.

  - spamassassin-tools: This package focuses on tools like "mass-check" and the "masses" directory, which are useful for generating rescore data from corpora.

This modular approach is really smart! It means users only need to install the specific packages they require, avoiding unnecessary dependencies. For example, someone who just needs the Perl modules doesn't have to install the entire SpamAssassin suite with its system-level configuration files.

I noticed this approach has already been adopted in the distributed .spec file, which is fantastic!

Theo, I'm curious about the evaluation test you've added to the tvd version of the

subject: Re: Difference between designer and OEM Polariod kit
body:
Peter Eacmen wrote:
> What's the difference between the Designer and OEM polaroid kits.  > Which one do I need to hook up the polaroid sensors to the HB?

The OEM kit is a great starting point for anyone interested in exploring the potential of Polaroid sensors with the HB. It provides the essential components - two transducers and two ranging circuit boards - at a very affordable price.  

The Designer kit, on the other hand, is geared towards more advanced users who want to delve deeper into the technical aspects. It includes all the components of the OEM kit, plus a bonus experimental demonstration board. This board allows you to see the raw data from the sensors in a visual format, which can be incredibly helpful for understanding how the sensors work and for experimenting with different applications.

While the HB itself does have some display capabilities, the demonstration board offers a more interactive and in-depth visualization experience.  It's a valuable tool for anyone serious about pushing the boundaries of what's possible with Polaroid sensors and the HB.

Just a heads-up, prices can vary depending on the retailer.  Some places, like Wirz Electronics, offer competitive prices on individual components, allowing you to start small and expand your setup as needed.  Others

subject: Paper 17
body:
This email is a fascinating exploration of innovative data sharing techniques! 

It starts by discussing BitTorrent, a widely used system for distributing files. While effective, it relies on a central tracker which can become a bottleneck.  

The next two papers delve into exciting alternatives. "Bullet" proposes a clever "overlay mesh" system that distributes data more efficiently and is more resilient to failures. Imagine a network of interconnected nodes, each contributing to the flow of information, making it much more robust than traditional methods.

"SplitStream" takes this concept further by using multiple interconnected multicast trees. This approach not only enhances efficiency but also ensures that even if one part of the network experiences an issue, the data can still be reliably delivered.

These papers highlight the ongoing quest for better ways to share information quickly and reliably, paving the way for a more connected future.

subject: OWB Articles: Important Article.
body:
Subject: OWB Articles: Important Article.

This is a collection of interesting and thought-provoking phrases and ideas.  It's a bit abstract, but I think it speaks to the complexities of modern life and the interconnectedness of seemingly disparate things.  

For example, the juxtaposition of "a turn signal toward the tornado" and "a flavored hell" suggests the unpredictable nature of our world, while the image of "a football team beams with joy" evokes a sense of shared happiness and community. 

The piece also touches on themes of power and influence, with references to "a CEO" and "an industrial complex."  It seems to be suggesting that these forces can be both positive and negative, capable of both great good and great harm.

Ultimately, this is a piece that invites the reader to think critically and creatively about the world around them.

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] Decimal(unicode)
body:
Terry Reedy wrote:
> The purpose of type constructors is to construct instances from reasonable 
> inputs.  I think all number constructors should accept bytes

What should bytes as input to a number constructor
mean, though?

People seem to be assuming it should be interpreted
as ASCII-encoded characters. This is great for simple use cases, but what about more complex scenarios where we need to represent numbers in a more efficient or specialized way?

An equally plausible interpretation might be
that it's some binary representation of a number. This could open up exciting possibilities for working with numerical data in a more compact and powerful manner.

-- 
Greg

subject: Re: Downloading
body:
Subject: Re: Downloading 

OK, attempting to answer this one now.

It seems like you're having some trouble getting your handy board to communicate with your Mac.  Let's try a few things to see if we can get it working.

I'm thinking it might be a problem with the serial port on your Mac, not the handy board itself.

Try this: download a program called Zterm (a terminal emulator) from:

  http://hyperarchive.lcs.mit.edu/HyperArchive/Archive/comm/term/zterm-101.hqx

Then, connect to the printer port on your Mac (you can do this by holding down the shift key while it boots up). Set your connection speed to 300 baud. Now, just type some random characters on your keyboard.  If the green light on the handy board's serial interface flashes, that means data is getting through. If not, there might be a problem with your Mac's hardware or software settings.

Let me know if you see the green light, and we'll go from there.

fred

This is a fascinating account of my travels and diplomatic efforts! I was particularly struck by the dedication of the book to King Edward VII, a man I greatly admired.  We discussed the importance of understanding a nation's history and culture, especially when considering its development.  

The construction of new railway bridges over the Vistula and Nogat was a significant undertaking, and I was pleased to see the progress made despite some initial resistance.  It was inspiring to see the enthusiasm of young British citizens, eager to explore new territories and expand British influence.  While I had some reservations about their methods, I ultimately supported their efforts to strengthen our nation.

My interactions with the Foreign Office were crucial in shaping our foreign policy.  We faced many challenges, including the Agadir incident, which highlighted the complexities of maintaining our position in Morocco.  I believe we could have achieved better results by focusing on strengthening our existing alliances rather than pursuing ambitious territorial claims.

My conversations with Admiral von Tirpitz were particularly insightful.  We discussed the importance of clear communication and the need for him to learn English to better understand our allies.

body:
##  The Career News

**February 25, 2008**
**The Career News**
**Vol. 8, Issue 8**



**The Latest News, Tips and Tools For Your Career**

**Blogging is good for recruiters**

Abridged: CareerJournal.com
MENLO PARK, CA -- Corporate recruiters have long surfed the Web to vet potential hires, but now they're also surfing blogs to unearth job candidates, expanding their talent pool and gaining insights they say they can't get from resumes and interviews. Most blog-related recruits are professionals
in technology and media because jobs in these fields often require knowledge of the blogosphere. In addition, recruiters say they check candidates' blogs about non-career related topics for evidence of writing skills and clues to how well rounded they are. Greg Sterling, a strategy consultant for Internet companies and a blogger in
Oakland, CA., describes job offers as "a natural byproduct of the exposure you get from blogging." He says he gets about 15 inquiries a month from companies and search-firm recruiters seeking to fill consulting gigs and full-time jobs. "My blog is a vehicle that keeps me exposed to people on a daily basis," he notes. Job

subject: Re: [ILUG] Strange ssh problem
body:
Hi Niall,

Some thoughts...

o Run the ssh client with verbosity 3 (ssh -v -v -v)
o Run an sshd with debugging level three (sshd -d -d -d)
o ~alfio doesn't seem to be nfs-mounted like ~alfred

(The following aren't really applicable, but...
    o Has alfred been logged in for ages? Has the tmpsweeper swept
      through /tmp removing alfred's ssh-agent directory in its wake?
    o Is /tmp a+rwxt? Does /dev/null exist?
    o Has alfred been messing with his TMPDIR envar?
    o What does lsof on his ssh-agent say? What about on alfio's?
    o What are the permissions on ~alfred and ~alfred/.ssh?
    o Does alfred's uid conflict with somebody else's?
    o Does alfred's $SSH_AGENT_PID correspond to his agent? alfio's?
)

Wesley.

subject: Re: [R] [R-sig-Geo] LIDAR Problem in R (THANKS for HELP)
body: Let's not cross-post!  I'll pick up only one point of general relevance on  this list (R-help). On Tue, 5 Aug 2008, Matt Oliver wrote: > you can try > > memory.limit(size=4000) > > only if you have 4GB of memory on the system > > This is not guaranteed to solve your problem though Assuming this is Windows (it looks like it) ....  The limiting issue on a  32-bit system is not memory (RAM) but address space: using  memory.limit(size=4000) will still only get you 2GB of address space  unless you use the /3GB flag.  (See the rw-FAQ Q2.9.) > With big datasets like lidar, you are much better off getting access to a > 64bit system with a ton of RAM (>64GB). Well, you are better off even with 4GB of RAM because of address-space  issues (and even with 2GB if the 32-bit OS is Windows -- I used to run a  dual-boot Windows XP / 64-bit Linux box with

subject: Re: [Webdev] PHP+Javascript request
body:
> er, I understood you until you reached the above. Maybe it's my lack of 
> PHP but why would a variable name _need_ to end with [] ?
> 
> With most languages you parse the submitted form data and should come 
> out with either variables or an array / hash, which you then work with 
> and name whatever you like.

I was just thinking about how to make our website more user-friendly.  I've been experimenting with different ways to handle multiple selections in forms, and I came across this interesting technique in PHP. 

IIRC, if the query string contains

varname=FIRST&varname=SECOND

then PHP will create a variable called "varname" with result SECOND. 
However, if the query string contains

varname[]=FIRST&varname[]=SECOND

then PHP will create an array with varname[1]==FIRST and varname[2]==SECOND

This seems like a clever way to store multiple selections in a single variable, making it easier to process later.  I'm still exploring the best approach, but this PHP method seems promising.

Dave


_______________________________________________
Webdev mailing list
Webdev@linux.

subject: Response to Beloved #2
body:
The character of Beloved is a fascinating study in the complexities of the human psyche.  She embodies the innocence of childhood trapped within a mature form, a poignant metaphor for the lasting impact of trauma. While her presence initially disrupts the lives of those around her, ultimately she serves as a catalyst for healing and reconciliation.  

Think about it: ghosts are often portrayed as vengeful spirits seeking retribution.  Beloved, however, defies this stereotype. Her return is not driven by malice, but by a deep-seated longing for connection and understanding.  She seeks to bridge the gap between the living and the dead, to find peace and closure.

Beloved's influence on the family is undeniable. She acts as a mirror, reflecting their deepest fears and vulnerabilities.  This exploration of family dynamics and the weight of the past is what makes this book so compelling.  

I'm finding myself drawn deeper into the story with each passing page.  It's a truly thought-provoking and emotionally resonant read.

subject: Re: great balls of fire
body:
Subject: Re: great balls of fire

Jerry and Mike,

Hope you guys are doing well!

The crazy thunderstorms last night and power outage got me thinking about how important it is to have our systems protected.  I was looking into ways to make sure everything shuts down safely in case of another outage, and I found this really cool tool called "apcupsd".  It can monitor your UPS battery and automatically shut down your computers if it gets too low.

I thought it would be great if we could set this up for everyone.  There's a helpful guide on how to connect the UPS to your computer here:

http://www.brisse.dk/site/apcupsd/

Let me know if you're interested in trying it out, and I can walk you through the installation.

Also, I'm sending up some DQS manuals to Richmond soon.

Best,

Arne

Whether you're a seasoned professional looking to expand your knowledge or a newcomer eager to break into the field, Natural Gas 101 offers invaluable insights and practical applications.  

**What You'll Learn:**

* **Origins and Destinations of Natural Gas:** Delve into the fundamental properties of natural gas, its geographical distribution, measurement techniques, and its historical journey and future prospects in North America.
* **Exploration through Distribution:** Gain a deep understanding of the entire natural gas lifecycle, from exploration and drilling to production, processing, transportation, and ultimately, delivery to consumers.
* **Buying, Selling, Marketing:** Explore the intricacies of the natural gas market, including industry standards, key players, pricing mechanisms, and the role of futures and derivatives.
* **Transportation: The Backbone of the Industry:**  Discover the vital role of interstate pipelines in transporting natural gas, learn about tariffs, imbalances, and the importance of storage in meeting seasonal demands.

Subject: are you infected with spyware ?

Body:

Did you know that computer security is a growing concern? A recent AOL study from 2004 highlighted some interesting statistics about online safety.  

They found that a significant portion of computer users:

*  Lacked up-to-date antivirus software
*  Were vulnerable to viruses

subject: Re: [opensuse] Intel 3945 Wireless Problem opensuse 10.3 - RESOLVED!
body:
Fred A. Miller wrote:
> I've been experimenting with different wireless drivers and network management tools to improve my home network's performance. 
>
> For a while, I was using the ipw3945 driver and the standard ifup method, but I recently switched to the iwl3945 driver and KNetworkmanager.  I noticed a significant improvement in my ability to connect to wireless networks, including some that were previously hidden.

It's interesting to note that hiding your SSID doesn't offer much real security.  It's more of a convenience for users who want to keep their network name private.  In fact, it can sometimes be helpful to broadcast your SSID so that neighbors can easily find your network if they need to borrow a connection.  The most important thing is to use strong encryption like WPA2 to protect your network from unauthorized access.  WEP is outdated and easily cracked.  I also recommend using a firewall to further enhance your network security.


-- 
Use OpenOffice.org

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] very bad network performance
body:
On Tue, Apr 15, 2008 at 1:19 AM, Guido van Rossum wrote:

>
> I'm working on optimizing the performance of Python's IMAP library.  I noticed that `imaplib` was sometimes requesting very large chunks of data from the server, up to 10MB.  I wanted to understand if this was intentional or if there was a more efficient way to handle this.
>
>  Could it be that `imaplib` was aware of the total data size and requesting it all at once? Or was it simply trying to avoid multiple smaller requests, which could be less efficient?
>

Well, it turns out that requesting 10MB of data is perfectly valid. The issue was that this large size was being passed directly to the underlying `recv` function, which then had to allocate and reallocate memory accordingly.


>
>  I believe most TCP stacks have a limit on the size of data they can handle in a single receive operation, around 64K.  Perhaps we should adjust our approach to request data in smaller, more manageable chunks, while still considering the user's desired size.  Something like requesting the minimum of the
